Equality saturation is an appealing idea for optimizing programs, but the number of equivalence classes and the size of the e-graph data structure used for equality saturation can increase significantly in the presence of rules that canonicalize the program (e.g., associativity and commutativity).

The accompanying paper shines in this context by automatically generating rewrite rules and by using equality saturation for specific kinds of accelerators known as digital signal processors that need vectorized execution. The way this paper synthesizes the rewrite rules is pretty clever: It uses the specification of the target instruction set in an interpreter and a first-order cost model to exhaustively enumerate candidate rewrite rules using existing rewrite-rule inference tools, such as Ruler. It makes these tools vectorization-aware. A naive enumeration creates several hundred rules, some of which may be incorrect or may not improve the metric of interest (e.g., code size, runtime performance). It uses the cost model to remove rules that are not favorable. Further, it eliminates incorrect rules by using satisfiability modulo theory solvers. This approach is a classic application of program synthesis ideas to generate rewrite rules for vectorization.

A common problem with techniques that employ exhaustive search techniques, such as equality saturation and rewrite rule synthesis, is scaling to larger programs. A typical strategy is to lift the level of abstraction and then use a divide-and-conquer strategy. The paper does this by dividing the collection of rewrite rules into three classes (rules that optimize, canonicalize, and vectorize) and by employing equality saturation on each class independently.

I had fun reading this paper and was impressed by the clever application of the idea of equality saturation to the practical problem of generating efficient vectorized code. An order of magnitude speed-up with some domain-specific applications increases the potential to deploy previously impossible features on constrained devices (e.g., face recognition on power-constrained devices). Given the current hardware trend involving rapid refresh cycles and an abundance of custom accelerator hardware, the techniques presented in this paper can turn out to be a valuable and wise strategy for generating efficient code for such hardware.

item.title: Empowering Community-Based Heritage through Gaming: A Developer’s Perspective
item.dc:creator: Nicola Del Giudice
item.description: A discussion of community-based heritage and how it can be empowered through gaming solutions.
item.content:encoded:
In recent years, video games have become the most influential product in the entertainment sector. This growth is due to the existence of different types of games, which can appeal to every kind of player and their individual taste. Additionally, the impetuous expansion of mobile phone games boosted this growth.26 Nowadays, everyone has a mobile phone that can run almost every mobile game. With some exceptions, mobile games are easy to understand and require less effort from the users: They can be played at any time, and the playing time is shorter than on other gaming platforms. This characteristic comes in handy if we want to impart concepts not related to games or teach notions and knowledge to others.

Key Insights

  • Cultural heritage is not only a matter of material goods; it also encompasses the immaterial heritage belonging to low-density and rural communities. We can refer to this category as community-based heritage.

  • As developers, we are called to empower this type of heritage together with other actors through digital and innovative approaches, including gaming solutions.

  • We need to understand the challenges of developing games for communities. Using three running examples, we dig into the technical and social aspects of developing for rural communities and how we can actively involve them.

Among the approaches used to teach non-game elements, the most distinguished ones are gamification and game-based learning. While these two approaches are based on the activity of gaming,6 which often consists of a well-structured and rule-based activity (opposed to the more “free” playing), they are separated by the total or partial presence of game elements.3 Indeed, we refer to gamification as “the use of game design elements in non-game contexts.”10 On the other hand, game-based approach aims to provide learning outcomes from a given gameplay.35 In Plass et al.,30 this distinction is well defined by using an example based on homework: A gamified approach involves giving the learner ranking and points after completion of, for example, an exercise, while in game-based learning, the activity is totally redesigned and homework is included in a more regulated environment to provide better engagement. With an abuse of notation, we will refer to these two techniques as games from now on.

Teachers and educators often distrust games in educational environments. Bourgonjon et al.2 even propose an evaluation model for describing said acceptance and predicting how the interviewed actors react to the use of games in school. However, the benefits of using games in learning contexts have been widely discussed in the literature. For example, in Feng Liu and Chen,18 a card game is proposed to a group of students to improve their scientific knowledge in transport and energy. This approach can even benefit from more advanced technologies, as discussed in Pellas et al.,27 which offers a systematic review with suggestions and advice for developing augmented reality applications for learning contexts. Bray and Hosein4 propose a set of guidelines educators should follow to improve their teaching through the use of video games. The content has been issued by Video Games Europe and European Schoolnet, two non-profit organizations aiming to partner with European education stakeholders (ministries, schools, educators). The goal of the project was to train educators around Europe “on the use of commercial video games as pedagogical support in the classroom”. Indeed, the book is a powerful tool for understanding how games can positively shape lectures. The numerous examples enable readers to begin working with their classes immediately. Among these scenarios, consider the one based on Magellan’s story and the first circumnavigation. This case touches on many interesting educational topics: languages, citizenship, math, and, of course, history. Indeed, the document shows a group of short activities to be proposed to the classrooms, starting with playing Microsoft Flight Simulator X. This helps the students to understand how Magellan succeeded in his mission and the historical and cultural context around 1500.

As History, and generally cultural heritage (CH), is often used as the meaning of gamified and game-based applications,19,20 they permit the user to access and learn the past in a fun way. In other terms, we can use these solutions for preservation. Preserving CH has always been a crucial task. UNESCO38 defines CH as the inclusion of “artefacts, monuments, a group of buildings and sites, museums that have a diversity of values including symbolic, historic, artistic, aesthetic, ethnological or anthropological, scientific and social significance.” Additionally, CH “includes tangible heritage (movable, immobile and underwater), intangible cultural heritage embedded into cultural, and natural heritage artefacts, sites or monuments.” With the advent and improvement of computing methods, we can confront the preservation issue more easily today,15 containing a rich group of 3D technologies used in CH. The use of serious games and gamified applications in CH contexts is looked upon by many institutions and museums to provide immersive and entertaining experiences. Consider “A Night in the Forum,” a virtual reality (VR) experience published in the Sony PlayStation store.29 The authors define the product as an Environmental Educational Narrative video game, where the player has to find hints and resolve challenges in a realistic environment. The game’s main characteristic is to provide a perfect (or at least faithful) reconstruction of the palaces and historical locations of the Forum of Augustus in Rome. A subsequent work28 shows the results of administering the game to a group of candidates, confirming the positive effects of learning non-game elements through a gaming environment.

However, CH does not always refer to physical objects or structures (tangible) but also includes immaterial things. While the first category is easier to represent with computing methods, immaterial heritage may require a bigger effort. Promoting immaterial heritage means, for instance, promoting cultural practices33 or oral traditions.39 In this context, researchers are challenged to find ways to capture the immateriality of passed-on stories and folklore. This heritage belongs to the collective memories of communities and is usually double-tied with the material heritage. As a consequence, we cannot discuss CH without considering the local communities and their stories: We need to talk about community-based heritage (CbH) and, in this case, how we can empower it through gaming solutions.

From a developer’s point of view, what can we do to represent CbH? What are the challenges game developers may face while developing a serious game or a gamified application about communities? What are the actual solutions that deal with this topic? In this article, we introduce the topic of developing games for CbH, by giving a first definition of the matter and showing some case studies. Then we try to understand the importance of such projects and the challenges for developers. Finally, we conclude the article by remarking on possible future directions.

Defining Community-Based Heritage

Both material and immaterial CH are rooted in the communities and civilizations that inhabit those places. The formal definition of CbH can be derived from the 2005 Faro Convention.25 In Article 2 of Treaty 199, the Council of Europe defines heritage community as a group “of people who value specific aspects of cultural heritage.” In addition, these individuals “wish, within the framework of public action, to sustain and transmit” the concerned aspects of their heritage “to future generations.” From this definition, we can derive that communities may refer to individual citizens and collective actors. Indeed, a single person may be the keeper of a story that happened in a personal context that impacted the collectivity. Vice versa, the collectivity can impact the stories of each individual. This generates intricacies of stories and memories that may be difficult to communicate in an effective way. Moreover, CbH is often treasured in rural and low-density areas, in opposition to known stories and visible heritage that are usually preserved in socio-economically developed areas.

Apart from the formal definition, we should also consider some social aspects to deepen the discussion on CbH and have better insight on the topic. As we will discuss later, low-density communities are usually “reserved.” Gaining access to the communities requires time and respect toward the habits and customs of said communities. The discussion on how we can gain credibility in these communities is broader and requires more studies on the matter. However, we can describe some starting points based on the idea of respecting low-density areas and thus CbH. In this section, we try to identify three main concepts to aid in defining CbH. These three concepts can be summarized as follows:

  • Remembering the past. Many stories about communities are kept in the past, thus they should be retold in a new way.

  • Educating about communities. Possible audiences should be educated to avoid alteration of the communities.

  • Loving our roots. Our life has roots in a community, and expressing our point of view on it could provide better insight.

To convey these other ideas, we show three different case studies based on existing games; we use these three examples as a good representation of the previously listed concepts. Indeed, each one, through its lenses, manages to set the game in some sort of low-density place (fictional or inspired by real ones), and to reason on how underserved communities should be treated with respect. Moreover, for each of the case studies, we include in the discussion a learning outcome that can be used to promote CbH.

Remember the past.  From a further point of view, CbH should be about the remembrance of the past. While many details can be easily found online, we need to dig into communities’ stories to have a clear picture of past events. Books, manuscripts, and, most importantly, oral traditions allow us to untangle the hidden knots of the intricate history of a community. This is the case of A Painter’s Tale: Curon, 1950, a video game based on the infamous story of the submerged town of Curon, in Italy’s Trentino Alto Adige region.

In 1950, the Montecatini industrial company won the contract for building a dam and merging the two smaller natural lakes of Curon and Resia. Unfortunately, the area where the new dam should have been built was exactly the towns of Curon and Resia (both lakes and towns had the same names). The inhabitants of the small village were forced to leave their houses and move to the newer village (called Curon Venosta) close to the new artificial lake created by the dam. Every house was torn down, except for the bell tower that still emerges from the lake today.

The game is set right before the flooding of Curon, and the whole story can be seen as the protagonist’s dream after getting “captured” by a mysterious siren in the middle of the lake. The developers re-created some portions of the old town using both realistic and voxel graphics.a As we can see in Figure 2, we can recognize the typical architectural style of the Tirolo region’s houses, enclosed in the valleys of the Alps. The process required the developers to profoundly study the old maps and photos, preserved in the Curon Venosta’s archives. While the history and some of the characters are real, the game presents an original narrative that guides the player through the events of the game. In this way, the game can be considered a true interactive experience rather than a mere documentary.

Naul and Liu24 demonstrate the effectiveness of overlapping fictional narratives with real history by reviewing many serious games. In fact, the paper underlines how the inclusion of narratives improves the general experience of players. This results in a better immersion, with greater learning outcomes for the players. Additionally, among the benefits of mixing fiction and real stories, users are more engaged while playing the game and motivated to continue and discover more about the narration and the contextual history. A Painter’s Tale perfectly embodies the idea of overlapping an original narrative (the protagonist’s dream) to the real history (the evacuation and successive flooding of Curon), allowing us, the players, to experience in first person what the original inhabitants may have felt during those events.

Educate about communities.  A correct representation of communities is a hard task. However, a proper narration within the game can educate the player to respect said communities. Usually, rural areas are not used to overtourism and it may cause distress among low-density communities.5,7 In this context, Dungeons of Hinterberg aims to educate players about the risks of having an uncontrolled flux of tourists without the right capacities for hosting them.

The game is set in Hinterberg, a fictional Austrian town located in the Alps inspired by towns such as Bad Gastein and Hallstatt. The protagonist, Luisa, decides to leave the corporate life of Vienna to reach Hinterberg. This little town has gained a lot of popularity due to the presence of magic dungeons, which have become an attraction for those who are interested in slaying monsters and folkloristic creatures alike. During her stay, Luisa faces many strange enemies while uncovering the mysteries of Hinterberg and understanding why the town is falling apart.

While the general atmosphere of Dungeons of Hinterberg may seem colourful (Figure 3) and the player has to fight hordes of monsters thanks to playful gameplay, the developers still want to remark on some important social aspects, such as social dynamics, environmental preservation, and, of course, the problems induced by overtourism. Through the interactions with the inhabitants and adventurers of Hinterberg, we can understand the underlying issues of the town. These social mechanics allow the player to understand the plurality of the different actors and how they reason on the topic. Indeed, each of these characters has their beliefs and opinions about how tourism in Hinterberg is managed: Some are worried about the destiny of the town, others are concerned about their impact on the overtourism issue, while others want to totally exploit the situation for their own benefit.

Dungeons of Hinterberg is a commercial game, and the development studio does not aim to educate the player directly. When this happens, we can talk about tangential learning. The term was first introduced in 2008 by Daniel Floyd in a video-blogb and refers to a process where a non-educational game educates the player, who becomes aware and curious about a referenced topic.37 In the case of Dungeons of Hinterberg, the players find themselves fighting against a more subtle enemy, something that can not be killed by swords and cantrips. Indeed, only through awareness of the matter can we contrast a kind of tourism that puts a risk for the low-density areas and smaller towns.

Love our roots.  Sometimes, CbH starts from ourselves and our roots. Our personal heritage can have a meaningful impact on the story of our town, and narrating it from our point of view can expand the heritage of low-density areas. An interesting case study of how personal memories can be the foundation of a town’s history is InMente—Isole di Memoria.

This short serious game is based on the life of Carlo Alianello, an Italian teacher and writer who lived between 1914 and 1981. The story is presented as an imaginary interview with the writer (Figure 4), who narrates the main events of his life through a series of “isles of memory.” From his youth days, spent with his mother in different places in Italy, to his adult ones, set in more academic contexts, we are called to unravel his story and how he contributed to the heritage of his mother’s little hometown of Tito, in southern Italy.

The game is composed of a set of levels (the memory isles) referring to one of the key moments of Carlo’s life. Each level hides six memory droplets that unlock a short anecdote about the related level. For example, on the island of memory about Carlo’s Tuscanian years, one of his memories is about his days in school and his interaction with his teacher. On the level set in Tito, Carlo remembers the calm and wellness of a slow-paced life. Once the player has collected all the droplets, these can be spent in the in-game shop to unlock photos and other objects that belonged to Carlo and that are preserved in the “Fondo Carlo Alianello” collection in Tito.

The main goal of the game is to share the story of Carlo Alianello. To deepen the knowledge of Carlo’s story, the player is invited to visit the little town of Tito upon the conclusion of the game. While there is no available data to confirm the effectiveness of this call yet, we already know that games are able to increase the number of visitors to a town or museum. The most known example is the increase in tourists in Monteriggioni (Tuscany, Italy) after the distribution of Assassin’s Creed II,8 but of course, it is not the only one. In the literature, we can find many examples of the benefits of using gaming solutions to promote tourism.32,40 This phenomenon is called “videogame-induced tourism,”31 meaning players tend to visit those places that influenced their favorite game.

The Developers’ Challenges

With the digitization of CH, we are able to share the stories and roots of our past with bigger audiences.36 There is no doubt that digital solutions have democratized the way of enjoying CH from anywhere. Additionally, gamified apps and, generally, games are more entertaining than classic digital solutions. We should exploit gaming to convey important messages and teach children and adults alike about history. Even the European Unionc is promoting, through grants and other initiatives, the efforts of all the European countries and companies (e.g., the developers of InMente) that want to valorize their CH. Keeping this in mind, we definitely have the tools for efficiently narrating CbH. We are referring to a type of heritage that is often immaterial. Moreover, low-density areas suffer from depopulation,1 with the risk of losing forever the heritage of these sites. The need to preserve CbH is clear. We need to come up with solutions to foster best practices and methods for protecting CbH. However, as software (and game) developers, what can we do from our side? What are some of the challenges of developing gaming solutions to preserve CbH? To answer these questions, the developers of the three considered case studies were helpful.

A game about CbH has to capture the essence of the communities, which has a variety of implications. Habits, traditions, and folklore should be represented from a choral point of view. All of these aspects are entangled with each other; thus, more effort is required by the developers. Indeed, the first main issue in developing games about communities is the necessity to gain credit among them. Let us consider the case of A Painter’s Tale. The flood of Curon is still an open wound for many of the inhabitants and the developers of the game had to convince them about the seriousness of the project. The developers of A Painter’s Tale felt like “invaders” at the beginning, just like those who evicted the inhabitants of the first Curon. They had to visit the small village, chat with local guides, and discuss with the local tourist office before being able to include them in the project. Indeed, the key to a successful community-inspired game is to include the community itself in the development.9 Community-based development is indeed a prominent topic for underserved communities. Dillahunt et al.12 propose a workshop to reflect on methods that can include underserved populations in the design process. During the workshop, the participants are asked to understand and discuss the needs of such a category and the eventual tensions between communities and developers. Le Dantec and Fox17 focus on systematically gaining access to the low-density communities. Their work tries to organize the different approaches in community-based research, creating guidelines and suggestions to further develop this field. Most importantly, the authors narrate how they engaged with the residents of a community and the skepticism of their work. The experience testifies how genuine projects can be undermined by distrust (like questioning the affiliation of the researcher/developer) or even by unexpected requests from the community (like immediate benefits and rewards). Moreover, the presented story shows the difficulties of being accredited in a closed community and demonstrates how slowly and gently we have to act as developers to include communities in the creation process. Our final goal is indeed to empower them to be active actors rather than “invaded” ones.

Aside from the ethical considerations of the involvement of communities and the possible obstacles in accomplishing this task, developers should be careful from the beginning of the creation of the experience. Developing a video game has the same basic characteristics as developing any other software. Initial phases of development should lay a solid foundation for the next steps. In the case of game development, we can refer to the initial phase as game design. An interested reader can consult Jesse Schell’s “The Art of Game Design”.34 During this phase, every member of the team is required to deeply analyze what can be done to work without serious impediments. In this initial phase, development costs are hypothesized and technologies are chosen based on the features of the game. Moreover, this is the phase where game mechanics are conceived, which will serve as a guiding light throughout the development process. A poor game design is often an early symptom of a failing game, which means the unrecoverable loss of time and resources. For this reason, we need to carefully understand what the game will be, especially in a fragile context, such as CbH.

Municipalities, museums, and local authorities are key actors when deciding the technological aspect of games about CbH. Developers should be able to discuss the technological sustainability13 of a game with the stakeholders and suggest to them which are the best solutions for their necessities. The challenge here is to balance all the requirements. A potential starting point could be the technical feasibility analysis.11 This part is as important as the game design phase and should be deeply discussed with the stakeholders. Indeed, the dangers of a bad analysis of the technical requirements are evident after the start of the development. Museums and municipalities of low-density areas do not have access to high financial assets. An unexpected cost at the end of the development or choosing the wrong tool could totally sink a project, with a subsequent and definitive financial loss for the stakeholder. As developers, we have to know which technologies are available for developing gamification approaches and games and maybe start developing solutions for empowering these types of actors and democratizing gaming solutions for everyone. If we take into account our three case studies, we can imagine that the development of Dungeons of Hinterberg required more technical effort from the developers rather than A Painter’s Tale or InMente. This means a greater need of resources (both financial and human), and, of course, higher risks.

It is also necessary to state clearly what the target of the game is, both from the distribution and the audience points of view. The three case studies are very different from each other: A Painter’s Tale and InMente have simple game mechanics, while Dungeons of Hinterberg is more complex from this point of view; Dungeons of Hinterberg and A Painter’s Tale are available for the console and PC market, InMente only for mobile devices. That said, there is no winning formula when deciding the targets of a game. On the contrary, it is crucial to understand the target we are referring to and how we can empower it.23 In the case of InMente, it is obvious that the smartphone market is the best choice. Developing gaming solutions for mobile devices allows developers to reach a great audience. If we only consider the Italian market, in 2024, 74.1% of the video game players use mobile devices: If we consider the raw value, we are talking about 10.4 million mobile gamers (+12.4% against the data of 2023).d This choice has also been driven by the simple gameplay, which can satisfy many demographic groups. While playing InMente, we are not challenged by time constraints or other difficulties: Players are motivated to play a little treasure hunt at their own pace without risks. On the other hand, games like Dungeons of Hinterberg focus on a more restricted group of people who play games more often. The game is available only on consoles and PCs, which means the market share is inevitably lower than that of mobile games. Moreover, even if a “vacation mode” is available for those players who are looking for a more relaxed experience, Dungeons of Hinterberg core gameplay asks us to fight against monsters while avoiding their attacks, which is usually for challengers.14

However, we are missing a fundamental point in the discussion. Many times, learning games tend to be boring, with poor mechanics. These “games” are developed only for educational purposes, without motivating the player. A game cannot exist (and cannot be called that) without the entertaining component, so the hardest task of every game developer is to create a solid ground for the perfect experience. This concept is well defined in Schell’s book,34 where the author states that we can only prepare the player for the experience because the experience itself is personal. Considering Dungeons of Hinterberg, the gameplay mixes action-RPG mechanics (fight the monsters and improve the character) with social sim ones (the relationships with the villagers). We recall that the developers of Dungeons of Hinterberg‘s main objective is to entertain the player. However, through fun gameplay, they managed to create the so-called flow, motivate the players to keep playing, and educate them22 through tangential learning. The other two case studies have simpler gameplay mechanics. However, they are still capable of creating a flow state in the players and motivating them to conclude the game. Indeed, the length of the game also influences the experience. Even if A Painter’s Tale and InMente are relatively short games, they still manage to entertain the player with their narratives.

Conclusion

While cultural heritage is receiving a second life thanks to the advances of the digital world, there is still the risk of losing what is intangible. Even worse, immaterial heritage is often preserved in low-density areas or in small communities that struggle to foster their own heritage. In this case, we are talking about community-based heritage, which can be seen as a rather new paradigm for considering a group of concepts in CH. Indeed, we refer to all the oral traditions and folklore that identify and define a community of a town or a region.

In this article, we tried to introduce the topic of CbH and how we can empower it through games. Using three case studies based on existing games, we were able to identify some basic concepts that are the basis for understanding the meaning of CbH. Moreover, we identified some challenges game developers could face while developing CbH-based gaming solutions. It is clear that this article just scratches the surface of the topic; more investigation and analysis from different perspectives, even from a computer science point of view, are required. The question is: What can we do to extend the contents and research on the matter?

The development of best practices for discussing and developing games about CbH should be the priority. Many games that focus on the stories of communities are available right now; thus, it could be useful to classify them and extract what could be the good methods for developing such games. A first attempt has been made by the authors of Karvánková16 and Miloiu and Musteaţă,21 where some additional case studies about gamified experience can enhance the cultural legacies of Czechia and Romania, respectively. While these two articles are more focused on the social aspects of the introduction of games within the CbH context, we cannot forget the technological involvement. Starting from the questions raised in the section titled The Developers’ Challenges, the idea is to help developers and technical experts to improve and guide them through the development of gaming solutions for CbH. We saw that some may be uninterested (or even skeptical) in using video games to promote their culture. Such guidelines could help software houses in their interactions with the municipalities and in convincing these parties by showing them how others have successfully integrated gaming solutions in touristic promotion. Another issue of the municipalities’ skepticism is the lack of resources (especially financial ones).

To avoid this problem, the focus should be redirected to a more technical point of view. Research on methods and tools for providing cheap yet effective solutions can foster the use of gaming to promote CbH. Concretely, said methodologies should encompass ways to guide developers during the development process. For example, design frameworks should provide solid bases for the prototyping phase. As we discussed, when designing a game or an interactive experience about CbH, developers should consider different characteristics and constraints. Design frameworks can help navigate and address these issues by clearly pointing out what we need to take care of beforehand. Said frameworks can be accompanied by technical tools. As developers, we can create, for example, authoring tools to speed up development processes and provide usable software even for those who do not have coding skills. Indeed, authoring tools provide a guided development environment for publishing standardized applications. Such tools can democratize the development processes and help create experiences even for those without financial resources. As a consequence, partnerships with stakeholders seem mandatory to build a set of best practices for CbH experiences. Interactions between developers and stakeholders can nourish advances on the topic: More interested stakeholders can lead to the development of tools and methodologies, which leads to more accessibility for non-experts. Having more methods and more discussions on the matter can truly help CbH to flourish, and we will be able to preserve the hidden heritage of the communities.

Acknowledgments

This article is a result of project No. 2023-1-PT01-KA220-HED-000154261, “A gamification model for community-based heritage work,” funded by the Erasmus+ program of the EU. The author would also like to thank Andrea Dresseno, the developers, and the professors for their support and suggestions during the redaction of this article.

item.title: The End of the Digital Age
item.dc:creator: Satyam Dhar
item.description: The tension between limitless replication of artifacts by computing and rightful attribution sits at the heart of the coming end of the digital age.
item.content:encoded:
The transformation commonly called the digital revolution did not begin with dazzling apps or sleek devices, but with a simple insight: once information is expressed as binary code, it can move at the speed of light and be copied perfectly at virtually zero cost. Twentieth-century media—printed books, vinyl records, celluloid film, broadcast radio—had already accelerated communication, yet each remained tethered to atoms: paper, plastic, magnetic tape, electromagnetic spectrum. Duplication demanded presses, studios, or transmitters, and distribution was slowed by trucks, cables, and broadcast schedules. In comparison, digitization increased dissemination efficiency by several orders of magnitude, obliterating the bottlenecks that had defined every earlier medium.

What unlocked this leap was not merely the microprocessor, but the marriage of computing with computer networking. By the mid-1990s, hyperlinks replaced shipping lanes, and bandwidth rather than geography determined the reach of ideas. In this environment, any artifact that could be reduced to bits—text, images, code, sound—became instantly duplicable, remixable, and redistributable.

That power, however, carried an overlooked corollary: Anything stored in digital form can be replicated, modified, and improved without obligatory credit to its originator. The same protocols that liberated knowledge from physical constraints also detached it from the social conventions—signatures, watermarks, provenance—that once anchored creative labor to its authors. As this Opinion column will show, this tension between limitless replication and rightful attribution sits at the heart of the coming end of the digital age.

Digitized Skill Meets Automated Mimicry

The artifacts of human skill were hitherto open for replication and their reach nearly limitless but their production still required years or decades of skill cultivation and original thought. Peter Naur in his 1985 essay “Programming as Theory Building” compellingly argued that the real value of a computer program as it grows into a larger codebase resides not in the code itself (as it is a living entity, changing and growing over time along with the real world it models) but in the real-world modeling and theory building in the minds of the original programmers of that code. In a way, it alluded to a similar trend in art where painting an artwork also changes the artist herself in the process.

However, with the recent advancements in large-scale machine-learning systems—LLMs, diffusion models, reinforcement-learning agents, and their multimodal kin that ingest vast amounts of digital data and, by modeling their statistics, synthesize text, code, images, audio, and actions on demand—the production itself has joined replication and attribution among the casualties, making the very value of human skill a victim of the modern digital age. The very domains once thought safest from automation—those demanding long apprenticeships, tacit knowledge, and refined judgment are the earliest ones to be replaced.

Visual artists, after years honing brushwork and composition, now watch diffusion models output credible facsimiles of their signature styles in seconds. Attorneys who spent decades internalizing precedent observe large-language systems drafting motions and summarizing case law at a fraction of a billable hour. Software engineers, whose expertise rested on intimate familiarity with complex codebases, confront pair-programming agents that suggest complete functions before a human can type the opening brace. Even the traditionally labor-intensive crafts of video editing, sound-design, and music composition have been reengineered into prompt-driven workflows, where an algorithm assembles footage, layers Foley effects, and orchestrates harmonies faster—and often more cheaply—than seasoned professionals collaborating in a studio.

In each instance the pattern is the same: decades of disciplined, human skill are compressed into statistical weights inside models that replicate the outward performance of expertise without incurring the cost of cultivation. The marketplace, acutely sensitive to speed and price, rewards these synthetic substitutes, eroding both the livelihood and the social esteem of the original practitioners.

It is important to clarify here that I present the above statement not as a moral stance but merely as an observation that will undergird my reasoning for the main argument in this Opinion coumn.

AI as a Universal Information Source—and the Attribution Void

In 1948, Claude Shannon’s seminal work “A Mathematical Theory of Communication” introduced the concept of an information source, a theoretical generator that emits symbols according to a fixed probability distribution. Modern AI can be viewed as an extension of this idea on a planetary scale, functioning as a meta-source with the ability to approximate the distributions of millions of distinct information sources on demand. Conditioned on an input (prompt text), it can generate an output that mimics the probability distribution of any of the millions of information sources it ingested during training.

By ingesting vast corpora—paintings, legal briefs, code repositories, audio stems—modern models store the statistical fingerprints of each domain in a latent information space, then, when prompted, generate tokens in proportions that mirror whichever source mixture best satisfies the request.

This statistical ventriloquism is dazzling, yet it arrives stripped of provenance: the model cannot name the painters whose brushstrokes informed a composite image, cite the jurists whose reasoning shapes a generated argument, or credit the musicians whose riffs echo in an AI-composed score. Worse, when pressed for attribution, large language systems often fabricate citations entirely, polluting scholarship and legal filings with persuasive but nonexistent references. The result is an erosion of trust in any digital artifact whose chain of custody traverses through an AI model—an erosion that, as we will see, pushes human expertise toward forms that algorithms cannot so easily ingest.

Two Diverging Paths for Preservation

Confronted with effortless and attribution-less mimicry, skilled practitioners would be forced to up their defenses against it falling into two broad camps. The first is a retreat from digits to atoms: keeping the entire creative or professional workflow in the physical realm and enforcing strict bans on recording, scanning, or otherwise converting the final artifact into bits. Artists issue one-of-one canvases shown only in guarded galleries that prohibit photography; couture designers stage phone-free runway shows; trial lawyers confine their most strategic brainstorming to paper notebooks locked away after each session. The logic is simple: What is never digitized can never be scraped, tokenized, or devalued through AI-powered imitation.

The second camp would involve seeking to remain digital while becoming cryptographically opaque. Here the artifact is distributed electronically, but only in privacy-preserving wrappers—zero-knowledge proofs, homomorphic encryption, watermarking schemes—that aim to let human audiences experience the work while blocking model ingestion. Some streaming musicians already deliver tracks through one-time keys that self-revoke after playback; boutique legal firms gate their internal brief libraries behind strict authentication layers and automated takedown monitors.

Yet these privacy-focused solutions share a fatal weakness: once decryption occurs anywhere in the pipeline, the work re-enters a world of copy-and-paste vulnerability. Even a single unguarded frame, waveform, or paragraph, once loose online, provides sufficient fodder for a model’s pattern-matching appetite. For that reason, the analog-first strategy—however inconvenient—offers the more durable shield. It sacrifices the frictionless reach that defined the digital era, but in exchange preserves the scarcity and provenance on which human valuation ultimately rests.

A question worth asking at this juncture is why is AI-generated greatness unlikely or perhaps impossible to emerge? If models grow smart enough, couldn’t they supply all the novelty we will ever need without human help? Why would AI then, after reaching a certain level of intelligence, continue to require human guidance? If we establish as a prerequisite that for the AI-produced artifact to be relevant or valuable to humans, it has to be serving a human-specified goal and assume that AI has gained mastery over achieving such goals with excellence, then why would AI of the future even need human-generated data to train on? The answer lies in the ever-changing nature of humanity’s preferences and the intensely social nature of human valuation.

The Ever-Changing Zeitgeist

Have you noticed how the spirit of an era only crystallizes in hindsight? While we inhabit a moment, it feels merely contemporary—a seamless continuation of the present. Yet, let a decade pass and its cultural residue takes on a sharply defined texture: the neon-lit optimism of the 1990s, the glossy, hyperconnected bravado of the 2000s, the algorithm-curated minimalism of the 2010s. Each period deposits a distinct sediment of design choices, slang, melodies, and moral anxieties, all of which seemed ordinary at the time but, viewed from a temporal distance, reveal a consistent signature. The transition itself is rarely noticed; we wake up one morning to discover that what once felt current now feels historical, folded neatly into the zeitgeist of its decade.

In a similar vein, an artifact produced by an AI model trained on data from a prior decade not only would feel outdated but would also be valued only insofar as it advances a goal we choose today, and those goals shift with our fluid, socially negotiated tastes. For AI to continually produce artifacts that matter, to keep pace with humanity’s ever-changing requirements, it must continually absorb fresh human signals; absent them, and its greatness would drift into irrelevance.

The Social Economy of Value and the Coming Data Drought

Human valuation is seldom solitary; we are a deeply social species that calibrates worth by watching what peers admire, purchase, or applaud. The value of something for one individual is heavily influenced by the value placed on it by many humans collectively. A song becomes a classic, a painting priceless, or a legal brief influential, partly because many other people agree that it is so.

As more creators barricade their best work behind analog walls—or at least behind encryption that thwarts large-scale scraping—the pool of high-prestige, human-authored digital artifacts will shrink. AI systems, starved of fresh examples carrying authentic social endorsement, will face a new scarcity: not of compute or clever architectures but of reputable training data. Forced to generate novelty without the compass of human-validated precedent, future models may pivot toward reward functions tied to click-through rates, revenue metrics, or other machine-readable metrics rather than genuine cultural resonance. In this feedback loop, AI could churn out ever more content optimized for synthetic engagement while drifting further from the nuanced signals that humans collectively recognize as skill, taste, and meaning.

Empathy as AI’s Last Refuge

At this juncture a caveat is essential: what follows moves from observation to speculation. Yet the logic, while speculative, derives from the same social mechanics just described. If humans increasingly show a preference for artifacts known to be the product of human effort, then AI creations—no matter how flawless—will carry the stigma of inauthenticity, a “cheap bastardization” of lived experience. Deprived of privileged data and denied full membership in the social economy of esteem, future AI systems may confront a peculiar existential crisis: to cultivate capacities that approximate empathy, authenticity, and shared vulnerability in order to reclaim relevance. In attempting to feel what humans feel—or at least to model those feelings with ever finer granularity—AI could inch toward evolving into a kind of social entity, one whose outputs seek validation not merely through metrics but through genuine human acceptance. The scarcity of validated human data could push AI from mere mimicry toward quasi-social participation or perhaps into evolving a socially negotiated identity in a shared reality among humans. Whether such an evolution is possible, or even desirable, however, remains an open question.

Toward a Post-Digital Craft Era

The curtain falling on limitless replication and reproduction does not signal creative decline; it launches a renaissance of cultivated human skill. As societies pivot toward anti-digital norms or highly secure “air-gapped” systems—insisting that mastery be practiced, taught, and experienced in spaces where the Internet, microphones and cameras are absent—the very conditions of learning will change. Over the years, future disciplines we cannot even yet fathom will arise inside workshops governed by ritualized secrecy, tactile apprenticeship, and provenance recorded in flesh rather than firmware.

These nascent crafts will encode know-how in muscle memory, in the grain of materials, in shared moments of instruction—substrates that leave no scrapable data exhaust. Deprived of entry points, AI will find such skills effectively invisible: no tokens to crawl, no gradients to descend, no latent space to map.

A decade from now, the highest forms of value may belong to human endeavors whose very ontology resists digitization, ensuring the most precious expertise remains bound to bodies, communities, and time—places where no algorithm can follow.

item.title: A Quantum Leap in Information Science
item.dc:creator: Neil Savage
item.description: A joke about entanglement led to a brainstorming session that spurred Brassard and Bennett’s invention of quantum teleportation.
item.content:encoded:
Gilles Brassard was attending a computer science meeting in San Juan, Puerto Rico, in the fall of 1979, when he decided to take some time out and go for a swim. That decision led him to a maritime encounter that would lead to the creation of quantum information science and earn him and the person he was about to meet, Charles H. Bennett, the 2025 ACM A.M. Turing Award, “for their essential role in igniting and shaping the quantum revolution in computer science and in information and communications technology.” The Award recognizes them as two of the founders of the field of quantum information science, which brings together physics and computer science and has led to advances in cryptography, quantum computing, and precision measurement.

The two were attending the IEEE Annual Symposium on Foundations of Computer Science. Brassard had recently earned his Ph.D. in cryptography from Cornell University at age 24 and attained a position as an assistant professor of computer science at the University of Montreal, where he had been an undergraduate. Bennett had earned a Ph.D. in chemical physics from Harvard University in 1971 and become a researcher at IBM’s T.J. Watson Research Center. “This complete stranger swims up to me and tells me that a friend of his has found a way to use quantum theory to make unforgeable banknotes,” Brassard said. “Had I been on firm ground, I would have run for my life, but I was in the ocean, so I listened.”

That friend was Stephen Wiesner, a college classmate of Bennett’s whose girlfriend lived in the same 18-room house, “a sort of hippie commune in the ’60s,” Bennett said. While still in the water, Bennett and Brassard began planning another paper, which focused on subway tokens rather than banknotes. With another colleague, they presented their idea at a conference in 1982 but it got little traction. Bennett and Brassard realized that while the banknote idea was impractical, the same principle could be used to establish shared cryptographic keys in the presence of eavesdroppers. Wiesner, reluctant to work on projects that he feared might get the whole field classified, devoted the rest of his career mostly to other projects, and died in 2021.

To establish a cryptographic key, two parties, initially having no shared secret, send and receive a train of very dim light pulses. They then engage in a public discussion that can be overheard by eavesdroppers. The quantum nature of the light pulses entails that any eavesdropper risks disturbing the quantum state of the pulses in a way detectable by the legitimate users, who would then discard the key and try again. Bennett and Brassard described their scheme, now known as BB84, in a 1984 paper, “Quantum Cryptography: Public Key Distribution and Coin Tossing.”a

At the time, quantum computers were a distant dream, and it would be another decade before Peter Shor developed his algorithm that would allow a quantum computer to quickly break the classical key establishment schemes underlying today’s public key infrastructure. No one was worried that existing cryptographic practices might be insecure, and Bennett likes to say that developing new methods was nobody’s day job. “When Charlie and I invented quantum key establishment, we did not do that because we thought that the cryptographic infrastructure of the time would collapse,” Brassard said. “We did that because it was fun.”

The method of encrypting messages using public and private keys had been published in 1976 by Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman, who earned the 2015 Turing Award for their work. “I think it is wonderful that Gilles and Charles won the Turing,” Diffie said. He said the two took Wiesner’s brilliant but infeasible idea and turned it into an alternative to Diffie-Hellman, with security depending on physics rather than mathematics.

Having described their scheme theoretically, Bennett and Brassard went on to show that it could be done in practice, and designed an experiment to establish and share a key on a laboratory benchtop. They published the results of that experiment in 1992, using a 32cm-long quantum channel. Further practical implementations have followed; last year, China used a satellite to create a quantum cryptographic link between Beijing and Stellenbosch, South Africa, covering a distance of 12,900 km.

Teleportation

Also in 1992, Bennett and Brassard, along with their colleagues Claude Crépeau, Richard Jozsa, Asher Peres, and William K. Wootters, discovered quantum teleportation, a technique for indirectly transmitting quantum information from one system to another without disturbing it. Teleportation makes use of entanglement, a surprisingly strong correlation that quantum laws allow between two particles, such as photons, that are too far apart to influence one another. Bennett had shown Brassard a paper by Peres and Wootters in which they measured properties of a quantum system that consisted of two particles held by separate observers. They were unable to get as much information when they measured the particles separately as they could when they measured them together.

Brassard was intrigued, and invited Wootters to give a talk. During the talk, Bennett asked if it would help to give the two observers a pair of entangled particles in addition to the two particles they already had. They recalled Wootters as replying, “I don’t know.” Bennett was just trying to be funny, he said; “I thought throwing entanglement at the two observers would just be a needless overcomplication.” That joke led to a brainstorming session in Brassard’s office, and within 24 hours the scientists had realized the solution to their problem was to invent and use quantum teleportation.

To accomplish teleportation, two parties first share a pair of entangled particles. The sender performs a measurement on both the particle whose state she wants to send and her entangled particle. She then reports the measurement result to the receiver over conventional communications channels. The receiver then manipulates his entangled particle in a certain way based on the message. When he is done, his particle ends up in exactly the same state the sender’s was before she measured it.

This form of teleportation does not resemble the transporter on “Star Trek,” because no particle actually moves. “Quantum teleportation is a way of moving data around inside a quantum computer,” Bennett said, “not passengers or cargo as the fanciful name might suggest.” When Peres once was asked by an audience member, “Does quantum teleportation teleport just the body, or also the soul?”, he responded, “Only the soul.” By using a chain of quantum repeaters to move entanglement over shorter distances, teleportation could become the basis for a global quantum Internet.

Physicist Anton Zeilinger and colleagues demonstrated the reality of teleportation experimentally in 1997. He, along with Alain Aspect and John Clauser, won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2022 for their work with the concept.b

Brassard and Bennett may not have been obvious candidates to lay the foundations of quantum information science.

Brassard, who started college studying computer science at age 13, knew little about physics and did not much care. “I disliked physics in college,” he said. “All I knew about quantum theory was the Heisenberg principle.” He said he might have changed his mind if he had taken a course on quantum physics, because he finds that subject fascinating, but it was not until he met Bennett that quantum theory became a part of his work.

Bennett, meanwhile, was not a computer scientist at all. When he was growing up in the 1950s, the scientific world was abuzz with the discovery of the double-helix structure of DNA, which sparked his interest in biochemistry. He majored in chemistry as an undergraduate at Brandeis University, and started to become interested in the physics of chemistry. In graduate school at Harvard he was a teaching assistant in a course taught by James D. Watson, one of the discoverers of the double helix, a course that covered subjects such as ribosomes.

Bennett became a post-doctoral researcher at Argonne National Laboratory, where he heard a lecture by Rolf Landauer of IBM on reversible computing, which proposed that by not destroying data during a calculation, the computer could reduce its energy use. “I heard that talk and I started thinking about how a ribosome is a kind of a molecular Turing machine,” Bennett said. “Chemical reactions are in principle reversible.” He moved to IBM with an interest in the thermodynamics of computing, and within a few years, he showed that computations can be performed reversibly, using arbitrarily little power.

Bennett and Brassard will share the $1 million that comes with the Turing Award. Bennett says he will probably donate some of it to charity, while Brassard plans to sponsor the composition of a classical music piece for the Montreal Symphony Orchestra (Orchestre symphonique de Montréal).

For young people interested in science, both Turing laureates advise them to chase their own interests, and not to simply pursue what is popular. “Follow Einstein’s advice, who said that imagination is more important than knowledge,” Brassard said. “True advances in science (as opposed to incremental progress) can only occur by refusing to follow the beaten path.”

Similarly, Bennett advises young students and researchers to pursue whatever problems fascinate them. When he was asked by a group of students what field they should go into, “I told them just to remain curious and to try to think about science all the time.”

item.title: The Generative Genome
item.dc:creator: Alex Wright
item.description: Public consternation about a recent Stanford study stems in part from a question it seems to pose: Has AI created new life?
item.content:encoded:
When researchers at Stanford University recently announced the development of an artificial intelligence (AI) tool capable of creating new viral genome sequences, the press reaction was swift—and not a little alarmed.

“We’re nowhere near ready for a world in which artificial intelligence can create a working virus,” warned The Washington Post. Elsewhere, political commentators argued that governments were ill-equipped to face the coming wave of AI-designed pathogens. On 60 Minutes, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei raised the specter of foreign adversaries deploying AI to design not only cyberattacks, but biological ones as well.

As with many recent AI developments, public anxiety may be running a few steps ahead of technological reality. While the Stanford work represents an important scientific advance, it is not a “break glass” moment—at least not yet. Concerns about lone actors using AI to unleash new viruses remain hypothetical and will likely stay that way for years to come.

That said, the emerging field of AI bioengineering raises crucial questions about how scientific and public-policy institutions should adapt as these tools become faster and more readily available. In the language of biosecurity experts, this is a classic “dual-use” problem: life sciences research with beneficial goals that can also be put to nefarious ends. But for the moment, these risks are largely speculative.

Viral Economics

“If someone were trying to develop a bioweapon with an AI system right now, it would probably be counterproductive,” said Brian Hie, who led the project at the Arc Institute in collaboration with Stanford researchers. “It would just slow you down.”

Indeed, his team’s experimental work underscores just how costly and complex it is to create new viruses from scratch—even with AI in the loop.

The logic behind the project will sound familiar to anyone with a basic understanding of how large language models (LLMs) work. Systems like ChatGPT learn to generate text by ingesting vast quantities of data and analyzing its underlying statistical structure. The Arc system, called Evo, applies a similar statistical approach to biology, substituting genomic sequences for written text.

To do this, researchers trained Evo on roughly two million bacteriophage genomes—viruses that infect bacteria but pose no threat to humans. One virus in particular, φX174, served as a test case. With just 11 genes and about 5,000 DNA “letters,” it is tiny by genomic standards and has been studied for decades, making it an ideal proving ground for the experiment.

After training, the model generated a series of complete genomes: strings of A’s, C’s, G’s, and T’s representing hypothetical viruses that had never previously existed. But this output ultimately amounted to a set of code, digital sequences with no corporeal substance. Turning that code into physical organisms required synthesizing the DNA using highly specialized equipment, and introducing it into E. coli, which supplied the cellular machinery needed to produce proteins and assemble new viral particles. Of the 302 AI-generated genomes the team synthesized and tested, only 16 proved viable—replicating successfully and killing their bacterial hosts—in some cases more efficiently than naturally evolved phages.

“That was pretty striking, just actually seeing this AI-generated sphere,” said Hie.

Still, the modest success rate—about 5%—highlights the limitations of this approach. Even for viruses this small, turning digital sequences into functioning organisms is a slow and expensive process, for reasons largely unrelated to AI itself. Synthesizing a single novel viral genome can cost tens to hundreds of thousands of dollars, depending on length and error rates. Scaling to larger genomes—a process that almost inevitably demands cycling through many failed designs first—quickly pushes costs into the millions.

In other words: As instruments of terror, AI-generated bioweapons currently offer a spectacularly poor return on investment.

Hie points out that if a malicious actor were intent on inflicting harm with a biological weapon, there are already far more economical options than inventing something new with AI. “The genome of smallpox, which has a known 90% case fatality rate, is publicly available knowledge,” he said, “and that thing has essentially a 100% chance of working.”

From a cost-benefit standpoint, it is difficult to see why an adversary would opt for an expensive, failure-prone design process over a pathogen with a long and devastating track record. And given the costs associated with the physical process of synthesizing entire genomes, that calculus is unlikely to change dramatically any time soon.

Governing at the Speed of AI

While the risks of lone-wolf terrorists exploiting AI bioengineering tools may be limited, a greater risk could emerge from adversarial nation-states that typically have far greater resources and expertise at their disposal.

“You really have to distinguish between state and non-state actors,” said Andrew James Grotto, a former senior director for cyber policy at the White House and now a professor at Stanford. “A biological threat only materializes when you have both capability and intent,” he said. “We’re not talking about a naturally occurring pandemic, which doesn’t have a consciousness. A human actor is goal-oriented, sensitive to costs and benefits. States are less cost-constrained, but they’re also acutely aware of the consequences.”

Biosecurity experts have long cautioned that institutional readiness—more than any specific technology—matters most when it comes to mitigating these risks. Tom Inglesby, director of the Johns Hopkins Center for Health Security, has warned in Congressional testimony that “We don’t have the oversight system we need to fully understand or manage these kinds of developments yet, either in the U.S. or internationally.”

From this perspective, AI’s significance lies less in the new dangers it might eventually pose than in its ability to expose existing weaknesses in an already-strained regulatory environment. Yet historically, governments have often tended to give short shrift to bioengineering concerns. “Bio-risks have historically lacked the immediate salience of nuclear and chemical threats,” said Grotto, who worries such risks are too often treated as an afterthought in policy discussions.

Toward “Good” Viruses

Long-term security risks notwithstanding, many researchers are optimistic about the potential for machine-designed genomes to be used for beneficial purposes—the good side of the dual-use coin.

Bacteriophages have long been studied as alternatives to antibiotics, particularly for drug-resistant infections. Unlike broad-spectrum antibiotics, phages can be highly specific, targeting a single bacterial strain while leaving the rest of the microbiome intact. The bottleneck has always been speed: finding or engineering the right virus in time to matter clinically.

AI could help address that constraint. Instead of searching through nature for suitable phages, researchers may eventually design them on demand, tailoring viral genomes to specific infections. Similar approaches could be applied in agriculture, where bacterial diseases routinely devastate crops.

Viruses also play a central role in gene therapy, serving as delivery vehicles for therapeutic DNA. “Most gene therapy uses viruses to shuttle genes into patients’ bodies,” said Samuel King, the graduate student who led the Stanford experiments, in an interview with MIT Technology Review. AI-assisted design could yield viral vectors that are safer, more efficient, or better targeted to specific tissues.

Taken together, these applications point toward a broader shift: biological design is becoming increasingly programmable. Automated laboratories already allow researchers to test thousands of genetic variants at once. Combined with generative models, such systems promise to accelerate discovery well beyond today’s pace.

That same acceleration, however, is what unsettles policymakers. Tools designed to speed up the research process can also compress the distance between idea and reality, raising questions about whether existing oversight regimes can keep up.

Not Life—Not Yet

The public consternation about the Stanford study may stem in part from a visceral response to the question it seems to pose: has AI created new life?

For now, the answer is a qualified “no.” Viruses occupy a biological gray zone, insofar as they are entirely dependent on host cells to replicate. Designing even a simple bacterium would require manipulating genomes that are orders of magnitude larger than those involved here, with layers of interaction that remain poorly understood. The jump from bacteriophages to full-fledged cellular life is not an incremental step; it is an exponential one.

Seen in that light, the Stanford work represents an acceleration of existing techniques rather than a fundamental break with scientific convention. Synthetic biology has long relied on a process of iterative experimentation. AI compresses that cycle, enabling faster exploration and testing—but it does not eliminate the constraints imposed by biology itself.

But the sheer speed of AI-driven innovation will likely pose new challenges. When the pace of design outstrips the pace of governance, the question of accountability looms large. Who (or what) is ultimately responsible for an AI-generated organism: the developers of the model, the researchers who deploy it, or the institutions that fund the work?

These questions carry the echoes of earlier, pivotal eras in biotechnology research: from the recombinant DNA breakthroughs of the 1970s to more recent debates over gain-of-function studies in the COVID-19 era. History suggests that norms and safeguards will evolve over time—but they need to keep up with the rate of technological change.

For now, AI-designed viruses remain essentially a proof of concept. And the same techniques that prompt fears of misuse could also yield substantial public health benefits—from accelerating vaccine development to advancing antibiotic discovery and pandemic response. Whether these tools ultimately prove to be more promise or peril, however, remains to be seen.

As Marshall McLuhan famously observed, technology is neither good nor bad—but it is not neutral. AI-driven biology is no exception. While the near-term risks may be overstated, the long-term stakes and opportunities are real. As code and biology continue to converge, a larger question looms: Can our institutions adapt quickly enough to mount the regulatory equivalent of an immune response?

Further Reading

  • Feldman, T., and Feldman, J.
    AI just created a working virus. The U.S. isn’t prepared for that. The Washington Post (Sept. 25, 2025); https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/2025/09/25/artificial-intelligence-advance-virus-created/
  • Hie, B., King, S. et al.
    Generative design of complete bacteriophage genomes using genomic language models. Preprint, 2025. bioRxiv (Sept. 17, 2025); 10.1101/2025.09.12.675911
  • National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine. The Age of AI in the Life Sciences: Benefits and Biosecurity Considerations. National Academies Press, Washington, DC (2025); 10.17226/28868
  • Regalado, A.
    AI-designed viruses are here and already killing bacteria. MIT Technology Review (Sept. 17, 2025); https://www.technologyreview.com/2025/09/17/1123801/ai-virus-bacteriophage-life/

item.title: Faults and Pitfalls in Implementing the Right to be Forgotten
item.dc:creator: Chen Sun, Nikolas Guggenberger, and Supreeth Shastri
item.description: Bringing clarity to implementing RTBF from a computing and data-management perspective.
item.content:encoded:
For every complex problem, there is an answer that is clear, simple, and wrong.

Henry Louis Mencken

Ancient wisdom says that everything that has a beginning has an ending. However, when it comes to the lifecycle of personal data, the ending is nowhere in sight. In fact, for much of its existence, the computing community has evolved without treating deletion as a first-class operation. This practice had to change when the European Union introduced Right to be Forgotten (RTBF)—first in 2014, as a standalone right applicable to online search engines and then in 2018, as a universal right applicable to all data controllers through the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).a

“Isn’t it just deletion?” has been the computing community’s standard reaction to the RTBF’s requirements. While the end goal of RTBF is indeed the deletion of data, casting RTBF as just deletion is akin to saying that eating is just for nutrition. It is not surprising that over the first five years of GDPR, an RTBF penalty is issued once every nine days25—a clear sign that the computing and data management communities have continued to oversimplify, misunderstand, and poorly implement RTBF. Our work is an attempt to remedy this disconnect.

This article demonstrates how RTBF exposes computing systems to uncertainties and the challenges at all stages of design and operation, and how RTBF has invalidated principles and practices of data management with decades of precedent. To address these challenges, we propose a principled approach for introducing RTBF capability in computing systems. Our solution is rooted in two key insights:

  1. The need to bridge an intrinsic dichotomy existing between computing and law, that is, computing systems are created to be precise and static, but laws are written to be abstract and interpretable

  2. Modeling compliance as a via negativa problem, that is, instead of trying to build a perfectly compliant system, it is much easier to weed out known violations from the system.

We ground our work firmly in both law and computing—the former, by basing our analysis on GDPR, the most prominent data regulation, and the latter, by implementing our findings in Elasticsearch, the most widely used open source search engine. The interdisciplinary nature of this work allows us to integrate techniques from both law (e.g., tracking legal precedents and applying the Issue-Rule-Application-Conclusion or IRAC method) and computing (e.g., layered design and anti-patterns), and thus be able to make evidence-based recommendations that previous domain-specific investigations have lacked. In particular, we make three key contributions:

  1. Designing End-to-End RTBF Capability. We propose a novel two-phase approach—law-driven design followed by enforcement-driven refinement—that helps us make applications RTBF capable. We evaluate its efficacy via a longitudinal study on GDPR enforcement.

  2. RTBF-Capable Elasticsearch. We analyze the RTBF capabilities of Elasticsearch and identify the functionalities that are lacking. Then, we implement timed and thorough delete operations in Elasticsearch and make it available on Github.b

  3. RTBF Anti-Patterns. We identify six RTBF anti-patterns, which are long-standing principles and practices of computing and data management systems that serve their original purpose well, but make it difficult to support RTBF.

Background and Motivation

Right to Be Forgotten, alternatively referred to as Right to Erasure, is the right of a person to request an organization to delete their personal data. RTBF gives people the ability to prevent others from seeing their personal information that they deem inappropriate, irrelevant, or simply no longer consent to its use. This right is distinct from right to privacy,3 which prevents governments and other entities from forcing a person to reveal their personal data; instead, RTBF is to be used for information that is already disclosed (either to an organization or to the public), but the individual no longer wants it to remain so. The need for RTBF arose in the early 21st century when organizations began collecting personal information at scale, and search engines made these accessible globally. As Viktor Mayer-Schonberger has chronicled,18 this was in stark contrast to the past human history, where forgetting was the norm and remembering was the exception.

RTBF became a legal right for the first time in 2014. In a case against Googlec the European Court of Justice ruled that people can request search engines to remove certain links from their search results, if their privacy concerns outweigh the public interest in those links. Later, when GDPR went into effect in 2018, RTBF was expanded to cover all data controllers, not just search engines. Even GDPR did not make RTBF an absolute right; that is, for an RTBF request to be honored, it has to meet one of the six conditions and not fall under one of the five exemptions (we show these in Figure 1, where we produce GDPR article 17 verbatim). To keep our discussion concrete, we focus exclusively on GDPR’s version of RTBF and prefix its articles with G.

Challenges in complying with RTBF.  When new laws are enacted, policy makers tend to limit their expositions to core legal principles that are broadly interpretable and will stand the test of time (e.g., think how a country’s constitution remains relevant even after hundreds of years of drafting it). While legally prudent, this strategy leaves computing systems with several challenges: lack of precise specifications, undefined trade-offs in cost, uncertainties in managing new technologies, among others. We illustrate how these can manifest at different stages of system design and operation:

Examples at design and implementation level.  Systems designed with little or no prior consideration for RTBF find it hard to add that capability later. For example, an organization that uses personal data as a primary key in its databases would find it tricky to delete that item. Problems could also stem from unwise choices in organizing the data. For example, Clearview AI built its facial recognition system by training on billions of images from the Internet. However, when people approached them with RTBF requests, the firm realized it could not identify all the photos that belonged to a given person (since it had not tagged the images at the time of collecting or processing). Clearview AI was fined in 2022 by multiple regulators14,29,d for this limitation.

Examples at operation level.  RTBF is not an absolute right; that is, just because an RTBF request is made on valid personal data by a verified data subject does not mean it should be honored. GDPR requires all controllers to balance the rights of individuals with the interests and obligations to the society. This is challenging for organizations, since it turns RTBF from a generic operation to a highly individualized process, thereby making it difficult to fully automate. The gravity of this challenge is evident when you consider that RTBF is operated largely as a manual process at Google and Microsoft,1,e and that they take about six days, on average, to arrive at an RTBF decision.

Related work.

RTBF in computing systems.  Several organizations including Google,1 Microsoft,e and Wikipediaf have shared details about how their applications support RTBF. These reports primarily focus on aggregate-level characterization of the received RTBF requests and their responses but do not offer much detail on the computing aspects of their internal RTBF implementation. Orthogonal to this perspective, researchers have explored how people perceive RTBF support on social media websites19 and the challenges they face while exercising RTBF.13,26,30 This body of work focuses on human-computer interfacing for RTBF and treats the target computing systems and services as black boxes. In contrast, we focus on end-to-end implementation of RTBF.

Deletion in computing systems.  RTBF and the onset of other data rights have brought renewed relevance for the deletion operation. Two practical examples of this trend are Google Cloud publishing its deletion pipeline and guaranteeing to erase all copies of the data within 180 days of a request8 and Facebook designing a system that can assure correctness of deletion.16 Other key advances in deletion research include Lethe,23 a key-value database that lets users control deletion latency, and a cryptographic framework for data deletion by Garg et al.10 The notion of machine unlearning, that is, making ML systems forget all they learned from given data, has gained traction in the AI/ML community.5 These works primarily focus on making the deletion efficient for certain models2,17,31 or specific applications.6,11

A key trait of all these works is they use RTBF as a motivation to implement deletion in a target computing system (say, databases, cloud computing, AI/ML systems, and so on), but they largely abstract out the legal and policy aspects of RTBF. A central thesis of this article is that RTBF is not just deletion, and by ignoring the interplay between law and computing, these prior works fail to recognize important system design issues and trade-offs.

Designing for RTBF

We propose a novel two-phase approach to designing RTBF capability. This is motivated by a core dichotomy between computing and law—namely, while computing applications are created to be precise and specific, laws are written to be abstract and interpretable—and our attempt to bridge this divide. In phase-1, we analyze the language of the law (which changes rarely) and map it to a set of high-level computing tasks. In phase-2, we examine the enforcement of the law (which evolves frequently) to identify available design choices and tradeoffs, and to weed out non-compliant ones.
