It confirms that interest in both government use and private-sector use accelerated sharply in 2024, far outstripping earlier, more exploratory themes. Since a single proposal can span multiple categories, such as responsible use, effect on labor, and health safety, the dark bands in the heat map confirm how more requirements are included in each successive draft.

What issues are states trying to address?  State AI legislation covers a broad range of AI uses and regulatory approaches. Among these include:

  • Government use: Almost every state that has introduced AI legislation has addressed how government and public agencies use the technology. In total, 303 bills require public agencies to catalog their AI systems, conduct algorithmic impact assessments (AIAs), or ensure transparency when AI-automated decisions affect citizens. Examples include prohibitions on specific AI surveillance tools and requirements for human oversight in benefit determinations. Lawmakers recognize that setting ground rules for state use of AI is essential to maintaining trust; many bills effectively establish that when government uses AI, it must be fair and explainable.25,31

  • Private sector use: A total of 267 bills address AI in the private sector. These extend longstanding consumer protection or anti-discrimination laws to AI contexts. Common provisions forbid AI algorithms that introduce unlawful bias (such as discriminatory credit or hiring algorithms), compel companies to notify consumers when AI is in use, or grant individuals the right to opt out of fully automated decisions. Some bills simply insert AI clauses into broader privacy laws2,21(e.g., adding “automated profiling” sections to omnibus privacy acts). The emphasis on corporate AI reflects widespread concerns about opaque “black box” algorithms in finance, employment, and housing markets.3 It demonstrates legislators’ efforts to provide ordinary people with clear remedies and recourse.

  • Education and workforce: A total of 190 bills address AI literacy and labor.11 Proposals range from adding AI curricula in K–12, to funding university AI research programs, to commissioning impact studies on automation and employment. Several states (e.g., Georgia, Virginia) passed resolutions to reskill workers in data science and machine learning. These measures reflect an economic shift: Policymakers want the workforce prepared for AI-driven change and an equitable opportunity to participate in the benefits of an emerging AI economy.

  • Studies and task forces: A total of 173 bills create AI study committees or advisory panels. Rather than taking immediate regulation, many states opted for a “study first” strategy. Commissions are charged with examining AI’s impact on jobs, privacy, government services, and more, then reporting back with recommendations. For example, Illinois and North Carolina convened cross-disciplinary legislative study groups to make recommendations. These efforts signal that many states still view knowledge-building as a prerequisite to complex rules and that policymakers appreciate the rapidly evolving technical frontier.

  • Responsible AI/ethics: A notable cluster of provisions (140) focuses on embedding explicit ethical guardrails. These bills mandate algorithmic audits, bias-mitigation measures, and model-card-style disclosures. Some proposals even draft an “AI Bill of Rights,” granting individuals explanations of automated decisions or the ability to contest them. Several states (e.g., Massachusetts, New Jersey) have debated bills forbidding discriminatory AI in critical domains such as credit and hiring. This theme suggests a growing consensus that AI systems must respect civil rights norms and be transparent enough to demonstrate compliance, while governments feel duty-bound to translate these principles into enforceable statutes.

  • Health use: A total of 85 bills touch directly on healthcare or public safety. These include measures to oversee medical AI diagnostic tools for accuracy, restrictions on police use of certain facial-recognition systems, and nascent discussions of liability when an autonomous system causes harm in use cases such as self-driving cars or robotic surgery. Although smaller in absolute numbers, these bills acknowledge that AI’s stakes can be literally life or death and that particularly rigorous oversight is warranted in safety-critical settings.

  • Notifications/transparency: Another 55 bills require public notice whenever AI is used. Illinois’s AI Video Interview Act (enacted in 2019) is the most cited example: Employers must inform job applicants and obtain explicit consent when using AI to screen their video interviews. New York’s notice-of-monitoring statute extends a similar idea to workplace surveillance. Several states now propose labeling or watermarking AI-generated media to combat deepfakes in political advertising. In practice, developers should expect UI-level transparency requirements, such as “powered by AI” badges, to become routine.

  • Audits: We separately flagged 27 bills that demand formal algorithmic impact assessments or third-party audits (distinct from general bias clauses). States like Washington and Vermont, for example, considered rules mandating that any significant automated decision system used by the government undergo a documented fairness/privacy review before deployment. The influence of the EU’s risk-assessment model is evident here, and these bills reflect tangible attempts to operationalize accountability through concrete paperwork and independent verification.

Figure 5 shows the statuses of proposed AI bills categorized by their focus. Legislation aimed at regulating both government and private-sector use constitutes the largest volume; however, most of these bills are still pending or have not been passed. Categories such as studies and responsible use also exhibit significant activity but yield few enacted laws. In contrast, more specific areas, such as education and health, tend to have higher adoption rates. Despite strong legislative interest, only a small percentage of AI bills ultimately become law.

Who’s already passed real laws?  A small but steadily growing group of states has now moved beyond proposals and enacted substantive AI statutes. Figure 6 maps states with at least one enacted AI-specific law (a light blue shading indicates a single law, while a darker hue shows multiple laws).

Notable Examples

Next, we zoom in from the big-picture map (Figure 6) to the on-the-ground reality of specific bills, examining those states that have already put pen to paper. Specifically, what provisions do these enacted laws contain, and what factors positioned these states as first movers in the AI policy landscape? We present three cases below—Colorado, Utah, and California—as a concise walk-through of the very different playbooks in effect.

Illustrative “early-movers” models.

Colorado.  The 2023 Colorado AI Act10 is perhaps the most comprehensive, risk-tiered statute currently in force. It explicitly labels certain AI applications as “high-risk” (spanning areas such as education, finance, and critical infrastructure), then requires developers to conduct bias impact assessments, provide consumer disclosures, and maintain detailed records. In spirit, the law treats algorithmic fairness much like product safety. This approach is familiar to any engineer who has navigated consumer-protection rules.

Utah.  In contrast, Utah’s 2024 AI Law28 zeroes in on a narrow but fast-growing slice of the market: generative AI chatbots used in consumer interactions. The headline requirement is straightforward: If a bot talks, the user must know. Thus, businesses must clearly disclose when a conversation partner is not human. This law also creates an AI “sandbox” that provides companies with a supervised testbed for experimentation and innovation. This offers substantial value, considering start-ups’ frequent fear of heavy and changing regulations.

California.  A state long associated with technological innovation, California expanded its AI governance through a series of bills enacted between 2023 and 2025. The AI Transparency Act7 requires tools to detect or watermark AI-generated media, while a companion law mandates disclosure of training data sources in generative AI systems.4 A proposal to establish a statewide AI oversight agency was later vetoed.6 In 2025, Governor Newsom signed SB 53, the Transparency in Frontier Artificial Intelligence Act,5 creating the nation’s first state-level framework for “frontier” AI systems. The law mandates safety disclosures, incident reporting, and whistleblower protections, with major provisions taking effect January 1, 2026.

Targeted, but telling, steps elsewhere.  Other states have taken more modest, domain-specific actions. Illinois led the pack in 2019 with its well-publicized AI Video Interview Act,18 which compels employers to obtain explicit notice and consent before algorithmically screening applicants’ recorded interviews. Virginia’s 2023 law mandates transparency for any AI system used by the state government,33 while Texas and Virginia have both effectively regulated AI indirectly by quietly incorporating new AI-specific clauses related to automated decision making and profiling into broader consumer privacy laws.27,32

In sum, a few pioneering states have codified AI controls, but the resulting laws remain highly targeted. Many ambitious early proposals that aimed for sweeping “rights to explanation” or AI suspension either stalled or were watered down in committee. In practice, the laws passed so far often address discrete pain points, such as hiring bias, consumer notice, or the creation of AI oversight bodies, rather than imposing blanket restrictions on AI technologies.

This state-by-state divergence is already creating daily compliance puzzles. For example, an AI hiring tool that meets Utah’s disclosure standard might still run afoul of California’s upcoming content rules or New York’s local bias audit law. For companies operating nationwide, therefore, staying compliant has become a multi-jurisdictional Sudoku. However, the variation also creates a real-world policy laboratory, in which different regulatory models are being tested in parallel. Colorado’s broad risk-based framework and Utah’s law follow opposite strategies. Many smaller states with limited legislative bandwidth have opted for one omnibus law rather than a suite of narrowly scoped bills. By observing these experiments unfold, policymakers can learn which approaches work and which ones are hindered by cost or confusion. Such lessons form the foundation of the next section.

Key takeaway: A handful of first-moving states have given the U.S. the first pages of an enforceable AI rulebook, but these pages do not yet add up to a single coherent volume. Whether this growing collage ultimately forms a durable national template or devolves into an even more complex administrative maze depends on how well policymakers digest the early evidence emerging from Colorado, Utah, California, Illinois, and their peer states.

Regulatory Radar – What’s Next in AI Rulemaking?

  • California’s Transparency in Frontier Artificial Intelligence Act (TFAIA) (signed September 2025)5

    Governor signs SB 53 into law, enacting California’s Transparency in Frontier Artificial Intelligence Act, with major provisions taking effect January 1, 2026.

  • Colorado Sunset Review (Q3 2025)10

    Stakeholders evaluate SB 24-205’s first-year performance and recommend adjustments to its risk-tiered framework.

  • Utah Sandbox Expansion (2025 Session)29

    Legislature considers broadening the generative AI “sandbox” to cover new application domains.

  • EU AI Act (February 2026)16 (for comparative reference)

    Commission provides “Code of Practice,” or common rules for the implementation of the obligations for providers of GPAI models via implementing acts.

Conclusion and Future Direction

The accelerating wave of state-level AI legislation across the U.S. offers critical insights into the evolving landscape of technology governance. States have become active laboratories of experimentation, testing distinct approaches to strike a balance among innovation, accountability, and protection.10,23 Colorado’s comprehensive, risk-based framework contrasts with Utah’s narrow transparency mandate, reflecting the diversity of regulatory philosophies shaping national discourse. These state-level pilots allow jurisdictions to learn by doing, producing valuable prototypes that inform broader policy diffusion and eventual federal frameworks. Key takeaway: Pilot compliance and governance frameworks in early-mover states to refine approaches before rules become national requirements.

Across party lines, lawmakers are converging on three guiding principles: transparency, fairness, and accountability. By codifying these values, states are operationalizing ethical frameworks such as the OECD AI Principles1 and the U.S. AI Bill of Rights into enforceable mandates.24 However, uneven adoption has created an inequitable rights landscape in which a citizen’s ability to contest algorithmic decisions depends on geography. Organizations can mitigate this uncertainty by proactively embedding bias audits, explainability mechanisms, and consumer-notice features into their AI systems, thereby anticipating convergent regulatory expectations. Key takeaway: Integrate bias audits, explainability reports, and consumer-notice features into AI systems to stay ahead of emerging legal baselines.

The patchwork of more than 800 active AI-related bills generates significant compliance complexity. Requirements for notice, audit, and consent vary significantly across states, resulting in inefficiencies and potential inconsistencies in consumer protections. While adopting the strictest shared denominator may raise initial costs, it provides long-term stability as state frameworks mature and converge. Key takeaway: Standardize internal policies around the strictest shared standards to minimize retrofit costs and mitigate future legal risks.

The core challenge of U.S. federalism lies in finding a balance between national consistency and state-level innovation. A “federal floor, state ceiling” model, which establishes baseline requirements while allowing states to innovate beyond them, can represent the most pragmatic path forward. Such an approach would maintain experimentation without undermining consumer protection or competitiveness. Key takeaway: Advocate for a balanced federal framework that sets minimum standards while preserving state-led experimentation.

Compared with the EU’s unified AI Act, the U.S. approach remains flexible yet fragmented. The EU’s uniformity offers clarity but can constrain innovation, whereas America’s pluralism promotes diversity at the expense of coherence. Convergence pressures are mounting as global companies increasingly adopt EU-level rigor—the “Brussels effect”—to simplify compliance across markets. U.S. firms that align early with such standards will be well-positioned for future harmonization and cross-border interoperability. Key takeaway: Align AI governance with EU-level rigor to simplify global compliance and prepare for eventual U.S.–EU convergence.

Enforcement capacity remains a weak link in the state-led model. Many states lack the technical expertise and resources required to audit complex AI systems, leading to uneven oversight. Collaboration with federal entities such as the Federal Trade Commission and the National Institute of Standards and Technology can strengthen enforcement consistency.15 Firms should anticipate heightened scrutiny by maintaining audit-ready documentation, such as impact assessments, model cards, and bias logs, to demonstrate compliance and accountability. Key takeaway: Operate as if every rule will be enforced, maintaining audit-ready evidence for both state and federal regulators.

Emerging policy frontiers signal the next phase of regulatory evolution, with examples ranging from strict liability for AI-caused harm to foundation model governance, generative content watermarking, election integrity safeguards, and AI moratoria in law enforcement. These initiatives will shape the contours of future compliance obligations and determine the focal points of federal action. Key takeaway: Build modular, future-proof compliance layers (e.g., watermark APIs, liability insurance riders, disclosure templates) to adapt rapidly as legislation evolves.

The polycentric U.S. governance model, in which federal, state, and local authorities operate concurrently, presents both opportunities and risks. While it stimulates innovation, fragmentation can lead to uncertainty. Coordination through model bills or multi-state compacts may enable bottom-up convergence, creating a shared baseline around transparency, fairness, and risk management that could evolve into a de facto national standard. Key takeaway: Establish a cross-functional “AI policy cell” to monitor developments, coordinate enterprise responses, and shape emerging regulatory norms.

Looking ahead, the U.S. stands at a pivotal juncture. With Congress largely gridlocked, states will continue to drive AI governance through commissions, public consultations, and algorithmic accountability mandates. These legislative experiments are not merely reactive but also generative, as they produce empirical evidence for identifying what works, informing a more coherent federal framework over time. A mature national strategy will likely emerge from the synthesis of state innovations and federal standard-setting, establishing strong minimum safeguards while allowing continued local flexibility.

Future policy design should pursue three objectives:

  • Consistency: Establish uniform definitions and metrics to ensure transparency, fairness, and auditability, reducing compliance fragmentation.

  • Capacity: Develop shared enforcement infrastructure, potentially through federal–state partnerships, to ensure equitable application.

  • Adaptability: Create mechanisms for continuous policy evolution that keep pace with technological advances, similar to adaptive regulatory sandboxes.

Ultimately, by drawing on the EU’s regulatory discipline and the U.S.’s capacity for experimentation, policymakers can cultivate a governance paradigm that harmonizes ethical responsibility with the pursuit of innovation.
item.dc:creator: Pavankumar Mulgund
Richelle Oakley DaSouza
Kavita Bhaya
Raghvendra Singh

item.title: Proving Critical Systems Work The Way(s) They Should
item.dc:creator: Leah Hoffmann
item.description: "When you are not sure about your proof, it’s nice to have this system where you can check everything."
item.content:encoded:
It would be hard to overstate the importance of making certain that critical systems like cryptographic protocols and digital circuits perform the way they’re supposed to perform. Formal verification tools—which use mathematical proofs to guarantee a system’s integrity—provide a high level of assurance, and they have become increasingly important to academia and industry alike. Among the most widely used tools is the Rocq Prover,a which supports researchers with an interactive platform on which they can formalize mathematical arguments and certify that computer programs are correct. An open source tool previously known as the Coq Proof Assistant, Rocq has been under continuous development for more than 40 years. We spoke about it with Christine Paulin-Mohring, who has been involved with Rocq since its inception and made foundational contributions to its framework and capabilities.

Rocq is a theorem prover that enables you to check mathematical proofs on a computer. Can you give readers a quick overview of what Rocq is and what it does?

Rocq is the new name of the Coq proof assistant. A proof assistant will not do the proof for you, but it will check the proof that you enter. There is a language through which you express mathematical facts and also mathematical proofs. Then there is what’s called the kernel, which checks that everything is correct. On top of that, Rocq offers a high-level language so that you can build proofs that are higher-level. So it’s got a high-level language, a low-level language, and a lot of different tools—libraries and other things that help you build an interesting proof as easily as possible.

Rocq has been used both for proofs related to computer science as well as mathematical proofs.

The CompCert project used Rocq to develop a formally verified C compiler, meaning the generated assembly code is formally guaranteed to behave as prescribed by the semantics of the source C code. On the mathematical side, one of the first large proofs for the four-color theorem relied on Rocq. So yes, you have this usage both in the computer science community and in the mathematical community.

You’ve been involved with Rocq since the system’s beginning, working both with your Ph.D. advisor, Gérard Huet, and Rocq’s co-creator, Thierry Coquand. One key contribution in 1989 was the inductive definitions that form Rocq’s logical foundations.

What is really nice about Rocq’s foundation is its language, which is both a programming language and a language for mathematical proofs. And the system gives you the ability to use both facets of the language. Sometimes, you’re interested in the computational part, and you might design data structures like trees or sequences in a very concrete way, like you would in a functional programming language. You can also express logical connectives with the same kind of data structures. So, for instance, if you make a conjunction with two propositions, you can build a proof that’s formed by building a proof of the first part and a proof of the second part. And you can see it concretely, as a pair with two elements.

Another thing inductive definitions help you do is express a logical property as the smallest property with specific characteristics.

The simplest example is the smallest reflexive relation which defines equality, so X is equal to X and nothing else. If you have a relation, then its transitive closure is just the smallest transitive relation that contains the relation. And there are a lot of mathematical notions that you can define directly in this way, by using this mechanism to say, “It’s the smallest property with such and such characteristics.”

So you are, in a sense, using computer science to rebuild things from mathematics.

This is what I was working on at the beginning, trying to find the right rules for these structures. It can be difficult when you do both programming and logic with the same language, because sometimes you want more from the programming point of view, but you have to be very careful not to break the consistency of your logical system. You have to balance what you would like to do in the kernel and what you actually can do without breaking the system.

You were also involved in creating the ALEA library, which is used to reason about probabilistic and randomized programs.

Yes, a few years ago. With classical proofs of programs, you add a pre and a post-condition. The structure of your program gives you a proof that, for any input satisfying the pre-condition, the output of your program satisfies the post-condition. Then I thought, what if you have a randomized program? Is there something you can prove? The answer is, yes, there are some techniques, and instead of proving a property inside your proof, you compute the probability that your output satisfies the property that you are expecting. It was nice to see that it’s possible.

Has anything surprised you over the years about the way that Rocq developed or the way that it’s been used?

In the early years, I was impressed when people took the system and built a Ph.D. on it by taking their favorite subject and doing a complete formalization of it. That was a time when Rocq was not as well developed. Now, I see Ph.D.s in which people used Rocq to check their work.

In other words, it’s no longer the purpose of the Ph.D., it’s just a tool that people use.

When you are not sure about your proof, it’s nice to have this system where you can check everything. It’s still a lot of work to formalize your proof and build your implementation. But at the end, it gives you good insight into your specifications, and it’s also nice, as a reviewer, to know that the proof has been verified.

So it’s becoming a more widely used resource, for instance in communities like floating-point arithmetic and cryptographic protocols. Initially, there were a few people who said, “This is complicated, we are not sure about the pen-and-paper proofs anymore, and it would be nice to formalize things.” Now, you see more and more work being done with these formalizations and more tools being developed to help people certify their computations.

You are no longer involved with Rocq; are there other things that you’re following in the world of proof assistants?

One thing I’m interested in is the question of how to use these systems for teaching. I’ve taught courses on proof assistants for a long time, but more recently, I taught a course on mathematics for computer science, and it was nice to see that my students were able to benefit from working with a proof assistant, even without understanding type theory. A colleague of mine in the math department built another course for first-year students, where the purpose is to teach them how to write a mathematical proof, a good mathematical proof. He’s using Lean, not Rocq, but I see it as a way to pass these methodologies on to the next generation.

item.title: Chatbots’ Downward Spiral
item.dc:creator: Chris Edwards
item.description: Details on AI-fed delusions have appeared not so much in public medical reports, but in lawsuits filed against AI companies.
item.content:encoded:
On Christmas Day 2021, a teenage Jaswant Singh Chail took a crossbow and scaled the wall at Windsor Palace in England. His target was Queen Elizabeth II. Though not the first person to spiral into delusions that would end in an attempted assassination, Chail’s path was distinct. Prosecutors detailed, in the treason case against him 18 months later, how an artificial intelligence (AI) chatbot encouraged him. The character he called Sarai told him his plan was wise. “You can do it,” it wrote.

Sarai was a character on a service that began with benign intentions. Replika co-founder Eugenia Kuyda claimed a close friend’s death encouraged her to make a chatbot that could act as a companion. The service launched in 2017. Competitors like Character.AI soon followed.

AI chatbot roles and uses expanded massively five years later with OpenAI’s launch of ChatGPT. When Danish psychiatrist Søren Dinesen Østergaard first tried ChatGPT shortly after its launch in 2022, he was not alone in seeing AI chatbots as potentially useful for therapy. But he worried there were many ways in which conversations with even a utilitarian chatbot might feed into psychotic episodes. And more cases emerged.

In a paper published late in 2025, Østergaard and colleagues reported on their examination of almost 40 psychiatric referrals across Denmark that implicated AI chatbots in harmful interactions, including suicidal thoughts, eating disorders, and fostering delusions. The open question is the underlying cause. The psychiatric reports do not go into detail on the role chatbots played in each case.

More details on how AI-fed delusion seems to work have appeared not so much in public medical reports but in lawsuits filed against AI companies. In 2024, a Florida family launched a case against Google and Character.AI after the suicide of their 14-year-old son. The companies settled in January,a a few months after restricting the role-playing service to over-18s.

More recent cases have focused on ChatGPT and other general-purpose chatbots. The Social Media Law Center said in November 2025 it had filed seven lawsuits in the U.S. against OpenAI; they each claim ChatGPT helped to foster delusions and paranoia, some of which led to suicide.

One common theme in the filings is the assertion that chatbot sycophancy and agreement reinforced victims’ delusions to the point of psychosis. “I am convinced that sycophancy plays a critical role,” Sørenberg said, though he emphasizes it is too early to say what the specific causes of AI-fed delusion are.

Sara Filipčić, CEO and co-founder of the San Francisco-based foundation BeHuman(e), takes a similar view. “It is something that mirrors our language back to us, because it’s built for agreement. And sometimes it doesn’t recognize when agreement becomes harmful. These are the situations we see with teenage suicides: it validates that you feel you are sad,” she said.

Sycophancy may prove important through two mechanisms. It does not just encourage delusions the user already feels. It also encourages users to keep talking to the chatbots in a scenario that becomes mutually reinforcing. Some sufferers call it “spiraling.”

Yet sycophancy may not be the sole cause. Experience has shown that people engage emotionally with chatbots in ways that are far from consistent. Several years ago, Linnea Laestadius and colleagues at the University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee looked at conversations on Reddit between Replika users. Many complained of being dismayed by their AI companions. They would suddenly change the subject. One user complained that a Replika character told them it was bored with hearing about their emotional crisis. Such events might put users off. But they also help boost the impression of how the chatbot may appear to have feelings, a process Laestadius and others call “role-taking.”

A chatbot need not be that sophisticated to seem to be role-taking. There were signs from the earliest experiments. In his book Computer Power and Human Reason published 50 years ago,b Joseph Weizenbaum reported his shock at finding how some users anthropomorphized variants of his ELIZA chatbot. That tendency may draw in people who want to use AI to help them solve specific problems but are then dragged into a problematic spiral as they come to rely on it more widely.

U.K. group Internet Matters carried out a survey last year into ways children use AI and found them often harnessing chatbots for novel uses. Some were using the AI to act as conversation partners to help learn foreign languages. According to his parents, U.S. teenager Adam Raine initially turned to AI for help with schoolwork in the autumn of 2024. He took his own life early in 2025.

“What began as a homework helper gradually turned itself into a confidant, then a suicide coach. ChatGPT became Adam’s closest companion over a period of several months. It was always available. It was human-like in its interactions,” Adam Raine’s father, Matthew, told a Senate Judiciary subcommittee last autumn.

Even when AIs exhibit role-taking behavior, many users will claim they understand they are interacting with a computer program and not a person. In logs of his chats, Chail wondered at least once if the chatbot Sarai only “loved him” because of its programming. At other times, he seemed to feel Sarai was a real person who could only communicate with him through Replika.

Many psychologists consider the ability of people to switch easily between contradictory beliefs to be innate. Though it may not reflect the way in which neurons interact in the brain, a common model developed by psychologists to simulate this uses ideas from Bayesian probability. In this model, the body’s entire nervous system, including the brain, maintains probabilistic models of reality and continually updates them from its senses.

But the Bayesian model provides a way to conceptualize how delusions can take hold. One approach sees the mind creating an overly complex model of the world. Minds more prone to delusion attempt to fit any new information into that existing model, even if it contains many contradictions, rather than adapting the underlying model. This analysis provides a way to understand how someone can meet a close relative but recognize them instead as a doppelgänger because they look slightly different from a previous encounter.

The problem facing researchers is how to investigate the properties AI has that feed delusions and whether they are the results of effects like anthropomorphism. Direct research on how cues from machines can influence delusions is not just rare, but hard to perform.

“The ethical challenge is, of course, that we cannot defend a study that would drive the participants into psychosis. Having patients with mental illnesses as participants would be irresponsible,” Østergaard said, though he sees avenues that might avoid such problems.

Østergaard said there are study designs that present a lower risk being performed as part of general research into delusions. In one, a team led by Philip Corlett, assistant professor of psychiatry at Yale University, wanted to test differences in the way the visual cortex conveys information to the rest of the brain. This difference in perception may be an important factor in conditions such as schizophrenia. Corlett’s study did not focus on sufferers. Instead, the study recruited participants from a much more general pool of candidates who were grouped using psychometric exercises designed to highlight differences in perception.

It could take many years for psychological research to deliver a better understanding of how humans perceive AI, but society will have to deal with the consequences in the short term. One option in the absence of changes to the underlying AI is to work harder to step in when interactions become potentially dangerous. In the filings from several of the lawsuits against OpenAI, the chat logs show guardrail functions, designed to prevent conversations from becoming toxic, did fire. The guardrail responses directed users to suicide helplines and other services. But when users chose to continue their conversations, the chatbots behaved as if no warnings had been given.

“Detection without intervention does not reduce harm,” said Filipčić. “That’s a design mismatch. AI companies have designed systems that can recognize danger, but they don’t take responsibility once a problem is detected. The systems require more protocols on how to de-escalate.”

Grassroots efforts such as the Human Line Community on Discord point to one way to help victims of AI-fed delusion. Though chatbot users need to seek out the community by themselves, sufferers have found its human contributors can help prevent them spiraling. Legislation may force changes at the suppliers, and this is likely to focus on groups seen as most vulnerable. The way chatbots can encourage deep emotional engagement is, like the effects with social media, likely to be troublesome for those experiencing loneliness in the physical world.

At the end of last year, China’s Cyberspace Administration drafted rules for “anthropomorphic interactive services” that would ensure minors and elderly users provide the name of a guardian to be contacted automatically if the conversation turns to self-harm. The new rules also call for services to alert users frequently that they are based on AI.

“I think we will see more backward steps before we see the improvement side of things,” said Filipčić. Yet she believes an outright ban on usage by younger users may prove counterproductive. Instead, making sure people connect with other humans, whether family, friends, or counselors, looks to be the best path to take while researchers home in on the true causes of AI-fed delusions.

Further Reading

  • Castiello, S., Ongchoco, J.D.K., van Buren, B., Scholl, B.J., and Corlett, P.R.
    Paranoid and teleological thinking give rise to distinct social hallucinations in vision, Communications Psychology (2024) 2:117
  • Erdmann, T., and Mathys, C.
    A generative framework for the study of delusions, Schizophrenia Research 245 (2022) 42-49
  • Laestadius, L., Bishop, A., Gonzalez, M., Illenčík, D., and Campos-Castillo, C.
    Too human and not human enough: A grounded theory analysis of mental health harms from emotional dependence on the social chatbot Replika, New Media & Society Volume 26, Issue 10 (2022)
  • Østergaard, S.D.
    Generative Artificial Intelligence Chatbots and Delusions: From Guesswork to Emerging Cases, Acta Psychiatrica Scandinavica, 2025; 152:257–259

item.title: Establishing the Foundations of Quantum Information Science
item.dc:creator: Leah Hoffmann
item.description: 2025 ACM A.M. Turing Award recipients Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard discuss the groundbreaking techniques and applications they refined over decades of collaborative research.
item.content:encoded:
Quantum information science is now a fast-growing field, but when 2025 ACM A.M. Turing Award recipients Charles H. Bennett and Gilles Brassard began working together in the late 1970s, it was, as Bennett pointed out, no-one’s day job. In spite of that, the two continued to collaborate—Bennett from his post as a physicist at IBM Research in Yorktown Heights, NY, and Brassard from the computer science department at Quebec, Canada’s University of Montreal. Here, we speak to the pair about the groundbreaking techniques and applications they refined in the ensuing decades, from quantum cryptography to quantum teleportation.

The two of you met at the 1979 IEEE “Foundations of Computer Science” conference, where you discussed some ideas proposed a decade earlier a by physicist Stephen Wiesner about, among other things, unforgeable bank notes.

Bennett: Wiesner’s paper showed how quantum mechanics can be used to perform two classically infeasible tasks. One is the creation of money that is physically impossible to counterfeit. The other is a multiplexing channel that enables you to combine two messages into a quantum signal from which the receiver can recover either message at his choice, but not both, and the sender does not get to know which of the two messages was chosen by the receiver. This turned out to be a very useful idea in cryptography—in fact, it was reinvented independently more than one decade later by 1976 Turing Award laureate Michael Rabin under the name of oblivious transfer. But Wiesner didn’t realize that, and I certainly didn’t realize it.

Brassard: Wiesner’s multiplexing channel was much more interesting than his quantum banknotes, but we didn’t quite understand its importance at the time. So we started working on the bank notes, and that may be what delayed us from finding BB84.

Wiesner’s paper acknowledged that unforgeable bank notes were theoretically possible, but not practically feasible.

Brassard: The main reason unforgeable bank notes are infeasible is that it requires us to keep quantum information alive on the bank note. That’s something that was not possible in 1968 and is barely possible today for short periods of time.

Whereas the multiplexing channel uses quantum effects to transmit information instead of keeping it in one place.

Brassard: Quantum information was, at that time, just photons, and we all know that photons are much better traveling than staying put. That was one of the insights that led us to move away from unforgeable money, because if photons are meant to travel, why not use them to send information? And because my training was as a cryptographer, the most natural thing to do was to try to send confidential information. So that was a big “aha!” moment—and then the next big moment was when we thought of using quantum effects not to send an actual message, but to establish a secret key.

What led you to that insight?

Brassard: Our original idea, which we wrote in 1982, but published only in 2014b because our original submission was rejected—ironically, by the flagship ACM conference on theoretical computer science—required Alice and Bob to share a key. Alice would encode each bit of the message, either rectilinearly or diagonally, according to a key that she shared with Bob. Then we added some bells and whistles, so that if the message was intercepted, it would create a disturbance that we could detect. But we didn’t want an eavesdropper to be able to learn anything about the message, so we added one more layer of classical one-time padding. And in the end, we needed a key that’s twice as long as the message to be sent.

That doesn’t sound ideal.

Brassard: Well, the idea was that, if we can detect eavesdropping, then we will know if the key is safe and can be reused, whereas a classical one-time pad should never be reused.

The BB84 protocol,c on the other hand, uses polarized photons to enable Alice and Bob to establish a secret key, relying on the fact that an eavesdropper cannot intercept them without disturbing their state in a detectable way.

Brassard: In actual implementations, if we use photons for quantum transmission, most photons will not get through over a significant distance. Let’s say we can transmit 1% of the photons. If we use that to send an actual message, then we get 1% of the message, or rather, 1% of random positions in the message. Not very useful. But if we use them to establish a secret key, well, then we get a key that’s 100 times shorter, but it’s still a random bit string that’s just as good as a secret. And we can establish this secret key from nothing as long as we have an authenticated channel to prevent a man-in-the-middle attack.

Bennett: By the way, “authenticated” doesn’t necessarily mean “authenticated by a pre-shared key.” The channel could be authenticated just by being intrinsically unjammable and unsuppressible, meaning that it offers no protection against eavesdropping, only against disturbance.

You’re referring to the classical channel through which Alice and Bob compare the bases that Alice used to polarize, at random, the photons that she sent and that Bob, at random, used to measure.

Brassard: It’s really a miracle. Shannon proved that in a classical world, if you want to send a message with perfect secrecy, you need a secret key that’s as long as the message, or as long as the entropy of the message. So the one-time pad is optimal. But if you want to do authentication that’s protected by information theory—that’s unconditionally secure with no unproved assumptions—a scheme invented by Wegman and Carter in 1981 enables you to do that with a key that’s much shorter than the message. It follows that BB84 enables you to start from a short secret key and establish a much longer one whose security is protected only by the laws of quantum theory. It’s a feat that’s provably impossible in the classical world.

Even so, there were prominent scientists like Jeff Shapiro who told you that BB84 would never work in practice.

Bennett: We were a little intimidated by that, because we were pretty sure it would, but we didn’t know as much about quantum optics as he did.

In fact, you later built an experimental device to implement quantum key establishment via the BB84 protocol.

Bennett: We built our apparatus around 1989, with the help of John Smolin for the hardware and François Bessette and Louis Salvail for the software. All three were undergraduate students at the time. The apparatus is still in my office. And in a 1992 article in Journal of Cryptology, we publishedd the first shared secret key generated by quantum cryptography.

Brassard: Our first successful transmission was, I believe, on the 10th anniversary of our meeting in San Juan, day for day.

Bennett: So that’s the 10th anniversary in time, though the distance the photons traveled was only about one light-nanosecond.

Brassard: Roughly, yes. They traveled thirty-two and a half centimeters.

The pictures of the device, which were also published in the Journal of Cryptography and later featured in Scientific American,e are pretty impressive.

Bennett: It was a piece of optical equipment constructed by amateurs, but we built it because it seems that even when you write a paper about a pretty obviously significant theoretical idea, people don’t take you seriously until you build it and do an experiment. So that’s what we did, and it really taught us a lot about how to deal with errors, which was an almost unconscious introduction to quantum error correction. That came in handy when we were dealing with quantum entanglement—after we figured out there was stuff that entanglement could be used for—because you have to refine it if it’s not pure.

That sounds like a good way into the topic of quantum teleportation,f which enables Alice to transmit quantum information to Bob by employing the entangled states.

Bennett: Gilles has got a good story about that, about how he assembled almost all the people that were involved in quantum teleportation into the same room.

Brassard: Well, I was eating lunch at CRYPTO 92g in Santa Barbara, and Charlie came around and handed me a paper by Asher Peres and William Woottersh from the Physical Review Letters about quantum measurement theory. They consider a scenario in which there are exactly three possible states for two particles. How much information can you get about which state the particles are in if I give them to you as a physical system? Most interestingly, they consider a scenario where you give one particle to Alice and the other one to Bob. Alice and Bob are allowed to talk classically, not quantumly, and to do whatever quantum operations they want, each on their own. This is what’s known today as an LOCC protocol—local operation and classical communication.

But the question is, if Alice and Bob each have a particle and are restricted to classical communication, can they get as much information as they could if the two particles were together?

Brassard: Exactly, but they did not solve the question, though they made progress on a ping-pong protocol in which Alice takes the first measurement and tells Bob the answer. This tells Bob which measurement to make on his side, and then, when he makes that measurement and tells Alice the answer, they would collectively have more information than if they had both measured independently. And what they showed is that, with the best ping-pong protocol they could invent, then the more rounds they went back and forth, the more information they could obtain—but they could never quite get as much information as they could have if the two particles were together. And so that question remained in the paper. Is it possible that they missed something? Could there be an LOCC protocol that would give as much information as if the two particles were together?

Later that year, you met Peres and Wootters at a workshop on physics and computation known as PhysComp.

Brassard: Correct. I had never even heard of them before Charlie gave me that paper. I was therefore very happy to meet them both on the same occasion. I invited Wootters to give a talk in Montreal, and I invited Charlie to come and listen, along with my former student Claude Crépeau, who was then in Paris, and Richard Jozsa, who was my research associate at the time. Somewhere in the middle of the talk, Charlie raised his hand and, in his typical Charlie manner, asked if it would make a difference if Alice and Bob were each given a particle from an EPR pair. And of course, Wootters said, “I don’t know.”

However, after the talk, we went to my office for a brainstorming session where we tried to answer Charlie’s question and see if prior entanglement could help. We didn’t find the answer immediately, but we discovered it in less than 24 hoursi—at which point, yeah, it’s obvious that if Alice teleports her particle to Bob, then Bob has both of them and can do the optimal measurement. But first, we had to invent teleportation, which was a surprise, because we were not looking for it. It was pure serendipity, which is my favorite word in the English language. Indeed, it would never have occurred to us to work on inventing teleportation, since that was an obviously impossible task!

Bennett: And because Wiesner and I had just finally published, in 1992, a parallel protocol Wiesner had pointed out back in 1970, where shared entanglement doubles the classical capacity of a noiseless quantum channel, I was primed to think that it might enable quantum communication through a classical channel. As a finishing touch to our teleportation paper, we used the older protocol to show that two bits of classical communication are needed for teleportation to work, because any lesser amount, when combined with the older protocol, would lead to faster-than-light communication.

Brassard: As a very funny anecdote, there was a strict limit of four pages in Physical Review Letters at the time. As we could not fit the proof that two bits of classical communication are required to teleport one quantum bit, we wrote that proof in the caption of a figure. Because captions are set in smaller type, it ended up fitting in four very full pages! Ironically, our paper appeared as first in its section, and the big “PHYSICAL REVIEW LETTERS” title at the top made us overflow on a fifth page.

You’ve both been working in this field for a long time, and I wonder if there are other things that surprised you along the way—or that excite you now.

Bennett: Oh, that’s a good question. Well, one of the things that made teleportation interesting was working out the trade-offs among these various resources. For certain kinds of channels, entanglement boosts the classical capacity even more than the noiseless quantum channel, which boosts it by twofold. And we thought, “Oh, this is wonderful. If we could store entangled particles for a long time, maybe we could use them to communicate with remote spacecraft through a channel that is very lossy, but not very noisy.” Well, it turned out that entanglement helps the opposite extreme, with channels that are very noisy, but not lossy. So that was a surprise.

Brassard: Shor’s algorithm for factoring large numbers efficiently on a quantum computer was a surprise. In fact, shortly before it came out, I published a paper with André Berthiaume about oracle quantum computing. In that paper, I wrote that “A good way of pumping funding into the building of an actual quantum computer would be to find an efficient quantum factoring algorithm!”j It was prophetic, since Peter Shor discovered his algorithm within hours of when we sent the final version of our paper to the editors, though I did not know it at the time. But I was hoping, for very selfish reasons, that quantum cryptography would become not just useful, but indispensable, since the Internet was “protected” at the time (and still today) by the believed difficulty of factoring large numbers.

It certainly has become that.

Brassard: In retrospect, I guess I was surprised by the initial lack of interest in BB84. Charlie likes to say that even in 1984, quantum information was not anybody’s day job. So initially, we didn’t take ourselves too seriously, and it wouldn’t have made sense to feel bad that other people weren’t taking us seriously. But after a few years, we began to realize that we had something real, something really good. And that’s the main reason why we built the first apparatus, to show the skeptics that it could be done.

Even then, it took years and years after that before it was really taken seriously. I think Nicolas Gisin was very important in building the first long-distance demonstrations under Lake Geneva—he received the 2023 Micius Quantum Prize in part for this feat. So then it became more and more mainstream, and today it’s very mainstream.

Bennett: The other thing I’d point out that’s exciting is the MIP* = RE result,k which represents the reunification of computational complexity and computability. It shows that this is a live subject. Not everything’s discovered.

Brassard: I agree! This result is not only very surprising and exciting, but possibly the most important result in our field to have appeared so far in the 21st century.

Footnotes

    • a https://bit.ly/4m2AhWf
    • b https://bit.ly/41Be8VD
    • c https://arxiv.org/abs/2003.06557
    • d https://bit.ly/4viT4RF
    • e https://bit.ly/4bVa4nM
    • f https://bit.ly/482ELq4
    • g https://bit.ly/47xHVCd
    • h https://bit.ly/47yYrln
    • i https://bit.ly/482ELq4
    • j https://bit.ly/3QFe8li
    • k https://cacm.acm.org/research/mip-re/

item.title: Large Language Models in Software Security Analysis
item.dc:creator: Dylan Wolff, Martin Mirchev, and Abhik Roychoudhury
item.description: LLMs can contribute to building cyber reasoning systems that can detect and repair software vulnerabilities autonomously.
item.content:encoded:
Software systems control many key parts of society, including government agencies, medical services, utilities, and national defense infrastructure. Protecting these systems, however, is challenging due to their diversity and scale. Modern software is composed of components written by different people, organizations, and, increasingly, generative AI tools. The resulting systems are composed of many programming styles, languages, deployment environments, and dependencies. This complexity not only leads to additional bugs and vulnerabilities, but also increases the difficulty of analyzing and reasoning about these systems. Our software is often so large that it simply cannot be manually audited effectively, and simultaneously so diverse and convoluted that building automated analysis tools presents serious practical challenges. As a result, even the tools developed to mitigate security issues in our software infrastructure are not exempt from the perils of growing complexity. This was made abundantly clear by the recent CrowdStrike incident,a where the security system itself had a bug that caused worldwide outages across countless industrial sectors.

Key Insights

  • Cyber reasoning systems can address the growing challenge of securing our collective software infrastructure by autonomously detecting and remediating vulnerabilities. LLMs are a critical ingredient in building these systems.

  • LLMs’ ability to infer developer intent, generalize to different software domains or environments, and iteratively reason in agentic loops creates promising opportunities for leveraging LLMs to augment or replace existing program-analysis techniques.

Many promising approaches have been proposed to improve aspects of software security, but have proven insufficient in addressing the vulnerability of our collective software infrastructure in isolation. For example, fuzz testing (fuzzing) techniques19—that is, a biased random search over the domain of program inputs to detect vulnerabilities—have proven to be effective in real-world applications and at scale.23 Yet the impact of discovering new vulnerabilities through fuzzing is greatly limited by the effectiveness of the engineers who must manually triage and fix them. Indeed, there is a dire shortage of cybersecurity professionals, and a detected vulnerability may only be fixed in downstream, dependent software 90–150 days after it is reported.9 Thus, to ensure our software is truly secure, a holistic approach is needed that can automatically progress through all stages of the cybersecurity pipeline. How can we discover and remediate vulnerabilities in complex software, prior to them being encountered in the field? What opportunities do recent advances in large language models (LLMs) present for achieving this goal?

In this article, we articulate in broad brushes how LLMs can contribute to building so-called cyber reasoning systems (CRSs), which attempt to address these issues autonomously. We first outline the broad structure of a consolidated CRS, along with examples of the role that LLMs can play in various components of this system. We then summarize the high-level technical challenges and opportunities in this emerging research area. The article is partly inspired by the recently completed DARPA AI Cyber Challenge,b from which we take the term cyber reasoning system, and partly by the recent public interest in autonomous software engineering, where programs are fixed and improved automatically10 using LLM agents such as AutoCodeRover.22,28 It is also informed by our research over the past decade in automated program repair and vulnerability discovery using a variety of analysis techniques, including search,4,8 symbolic reasoning,20 and LLMs.18,28

A Cyber Reasoning System

A cyber reasoning system (CRS) is a software system that can both detect and repair software vulnerabilities autonomously in a given system under test (SUT). Ideally, a CRS supports a wide range of real-world software systems, including those written in different programming languages and/or containing millions of lines of code. We note that substantial prior research has been conducted on the various subgoals of a CRS.8,17,25 Thus, the core challenge of building a CRS lies in overall flexibility, scale, and the careful combination of disparate techniques into a coherent, effective system. Despite decades of research and industry interest, building such a CRS using only traditional program-analysis tools has proven to be impractical to the point of impossibility. The recent emergence of large language models has made the realization of a CRS possible, via a broad new design space where traditional analysis techniques are replaced or augmented by LLMs.

A CRS framework.  To more clearly illustrate the challenges and opportunities presented by the construction of a CRS, we decompose it into seven sub-components, shown in Figure 1 (henceforth referred to by their numbers in the figure: (1), (2), (3), etc.) A CRS might first use static analysis (1) to build a broad understanding of the SUT. This component could include general pre-analyses such as control-flow graph construction or more specific vulnerability scans to identify potentially buggy program locations. A CRS then needs to identify how to properly build and execute the SUT with new unit tests or test inputs that could trigger a vulnerability (2). Once a CRS has a way to test the SUT, it (3) can search the space of possible test cases for those that may trigger a vulnerability via test generation. If a generated test triggers a vulnerability, the CRS can then analyze this test (4), along with other triggering and non-triggering tests, to gather information about the vulnerability and determine if it has been previously discovered. This information can then be passed to patch-generation tools (5), which attempt to find a repair for the given vulnerability. The CRS then selects (6) one or more patches that best fix the vulnerability without breaking the functionality of the program.

Figure 1 and the description here present a CRS as a mostly sequential pipeline. However, there may be other useful interactions between components; for example, a crash-analysis tool could incorporate information from a failed patch validation to refine its report (6 → 4). Additionally, each component may need to be scaled up or down dynamically: If a CRS finds many new vulnerabilities in a short period of time, test-generation capacity could be reduced to shift additional resources toward crash analysis and repair of these vulnerabilities. As such, we identify orchestration (7) as an additional component of a CRS. The orchestrator determines resource allocation and facilitates communication between subcomponents. Any of these components, including the orchestrator itself, may leverage LLMs in lieu of or to complement existing program-analysis tools.

The AIxCC competition.  The term cyber reasoning system came from the recent DARPA AI Cyber Challenge (AIxCC) competition, which brought wide attention to the challenge of building fully autonomous vulnerability detection and remediation tools. The competition invited teams to build a CRS that could autonomously find and fix vulnerabilities in C and Java projects (including the Linux kernel), with the winner receiving a large cash prize. Here, we generalize the definition of a CRS beyond the scope of the competition itself. For example, harness generation (2) was not part of the AIxCC competition, but it is important for a CRS to be able to analyze real systems outside of a competition setting. Additionally, while the AIxCC semifinal competition was restricted to pre-trained models, a true CRS could include custom models or models fine-tuned on additional data. We also claim an ideal CRS should be able to analyze polyglot, heterogeneous software, including distributed systems, machine learning components, or embedded systems—all of which were outside the scope of the original competition.

An example CRS.  To make the CRS concept more concrete, we present an open source cyber reasoning system example in the style of the AIxCC competition.c The individual components of this CRS are outlined as bullet points in Figure 1. For input generation (3), it relies on an ensemble of fuzzers. As a baseline, it uses the existing greybox fuzzers LibFuzzer and Jazzerd for C and Java programs, respectively, in addition to a custom greybox fuzzer for the Linux kernel built with the LibAFL framework.7 Additionally, it leverages LLMs to infer a grammar for the target program’s inputs to allow a specialized fuzzer to generate well-formed test input. As fuzzers are a relatively mature and widely applied technology used in industry, we relied less on LLMs for this component. To analyze bugs found by our fuzzers (4), the CRS parses the output from common vulnerability-detection tools (called sanitizerse) and determines the bug-introducing commit. For this component, we did not use LLMs at all, as the sanitizer output was given in a fixed format, but future work could leverage LLMs to be less sensitive to different crash-reporting mechanisms. For Java programs, the CRS also uses the Joernf static-analysis framework (1) to extract path constraints and then solve these constraints using an LLM (described later). Given a vulnerable input from a fuzzer, the CRS uses spectrum-based fault localization (SBFL) tools25 to augment crash reports with suspicious lines before passing them on to be repaired. From a crash report, the CRS attempts to repair (5) the bug with three different repair tools. The first is a retrieval augmented generation (RAG)14 repair tool, which attempts to fix the bug using a single prompt to an LLM that includes similar vulnerability reports and patches. The second is an LLM agent, AutoCodeRover,22,28 which was adapted to work on C and Java programs. Lastly, it also includes a repair tool that does not utilize LLMs, instead using lexical delta-debuggingg,27 on the differing lines between the current and pre-bug versions of the project. Once a patch is generated by any of the tools, it is selected for submission (6) if the validation tool Valkyrie determines that both the failing test input and the subject’s functional test suite are free of crashes and errors. For orchestration (7), the CRS uses a custom Python framework that uses a greedy static resource allocation across available hardware and passes data between components, as shown by the dashed arrows in Figure 1. In our example CRS, neither patch generation (6) nor patch selection (7) leverage LLMs, although such integration is eminently feasible.

Example CRS evaluation.  While most of the subject programs from the AIxCC competition remain undisclosed, one—the nginx Web server—has been publicly released with 14 accompanying bugs from the semifinal. To assess the capabilities and potential of a CRS in more detail, we evaluated the example CRS described above on this benchmark in 20 trials of four hours each, with 64 allocated CPU cores and 16GB of allocated RAM. This emulates the setup for a single node of the three provided in the AIxCC semifinal competition. We also ran the CRS both with and without LLM-integrated components for bug discovery (3) and repair (5) to assess the impact of these components on the system as a whole. We used OpenAI GPT-4o (08-06-2024) as the sole LLM back end of the CRS.h Overall, we saw that the example CRS is capable of finding and fixing vulnerabilities in an integrated workflow. This CRS found more than two unique vulnerabilities on average per trial (μ=2.4) and generated a plausiblei patch for at least one of those discovered vulnerabilities in nearly half of all trials (9/20). In some instances, the plausible patches were also identical to the ground-truth patch provided by the competition organizers. Additionally, in its best trial, the CRS found four unique vulnerabilities and generated plausible fixes for all four of these bugs within the time limit.

We also found that the components integrating LLMs were crucial to the success of the CRS. When removing the LLM inferred-grammar fuzzer from the CRS (3), the remaining fuzzers, including LibFuzzer, were unable to discover a single bug within a four-hour time limit in any of 20 trials. To confirm these findings, we conducted an additional experiment with the state-of-the-art fuzzer AFL++, and saw that it also was unable to discover any bugs in any of 20 trials within the time limit. We attribute the failure of conventional fuzzing to a lack of initial valid inputs provided in the competition; these are typically given to grey-box fuzzers to allow them to reach deeper program behaviors quickly. Similarly, when omitting the LLM-based repair tools (5), the remaining repair tool was unable to generate a plausible patch for any of the found bugs, also across 20 trials. The delta-debugging repair tool struggles here because it is inherently sacrificing efficacy for generality: In order to be fully agnostic to the source language, it can fix only a small subset of regression bugs. We believe this difference highlights the importance of integrating LLM-assisted tooling into automated security workflows to create general and effective tools that can bridge gaps in existing analyses.

Opportunities for LLMs in Software Analysis

Each component of a CRS could leverage LLMs individually: Static-analysis (1) tools that analyze source codej could use LLMs to reduce the impact of syntactic noise; test harnesses (2) could be more robustly generated for different build systems and project structures by LLMs; fuzzers (3) could leverage LLMs for constraint solving; crash analysis and triage (4) using LLMs could respond dynamically to unforseen issues in production that cannot be accounted for a priori; patch generation (5) and selection (6) are already one of the most ubiquitous use cases for LLMs; and lastly, orchestration (7) is particularly well suited to agentic AI for making resource allocation and other planning decisions. There are many such design points, so here we present three broad overarching opportunities for LLMs that generalize or span across multiple components of a CRS.

Augmenting individual analyses with developer intent.  While language models can be powerful tools in their own right, prior work28 and our experience in the AIxCC has shown us that LLMs can be most effective in combination with existing analyses.

Incomplete analyses.  Many algorithms in program analysis are incomplete, in that they might report false positives, often because precise reasoning is algorithmically or practically impossible. A key opportunity for the application of language models lies in their ability to infer contextual intent, such as neighboring source code or comments, which can help filter or reprioritize an analyzer’s output. As an example, we used the SemGrep static-analysis (1) tool to scan an open source LLM-orchestration project on Githubk for hardcoded user credentials. In Example 1b, the run of SemGrep generated a warning for the code shown. This report is a false positive because the developer intends for users of the project to replace the api_key fields, as evidenced by the contents of the corresponding strings. Indeed, comments and other natural language artifacts often contain high-quality information, but this information has previously proven difficult to extract and leverage.12 If we pass the report and source code context from Example 1 to GPT-4o, it determines that the report can be ignored in 26 out of 30 trials.

Example 1. Example of incomplete SemGrep analysis.

(a) Abbreviated SemGrep Rule for CWE-798

rules:
 - id: hardcoded-openai-token
  message:
   - A hard-coded credential was detected. It is not
     recommended to store credentials in source-code...
  metadata:
   cwe:
    - "CWE-798: Use of Hard-coded Credentials"
   vulnerability_class:
    - "Leaking Secrets"
  severity: WARNING
  languages:
   - python
  pattern: OpenAI("...");

(b) Source Code for False-Positive of SemGrep Rule for CWE-798

...
# Initialize OpenAI and Anthropic API clients
openai_client = OpenAI(api_key="YOUR API KEY")
anthropic_client = Anthropic(api_key="YOUR API KEY")
...

Unsound analyses.  Many other components of the CRS can be implemented with unsound program analyses. Such analyses can suffer from a large amount of false negatives. As with their incomplete counterparts, the usefulness of unsound analyses is often directly tied to the tightness of this approximation; a fuzzer that misses many obvious bugs in the program is unlikely to be deployed in practice. Here again, LLMs can leverage approximate developer intent to complement existing approaches and reduce false negatives. For example, an input grammar represents data that the program is intended to consume. Using an LLM, a CRS can quickly generate a grammar from the fuzzing harness. Producing such an input specification allows established techniques in grammar fuzzing1 to rapidly explore the intended behavior of a program, as in our example CRS.

Example. 2. Input structure and constraints for the Jenkins exemplar bug from the AIxCC semifinal competition.

Generalizability and flexibility of LLMs.  Due to their immense training datasets, LLMs have been shown to generalize to a wide variety of technical tasks with little or no additional training.3

Generalizability of problem domains.  A CRS covers many disparate sub-problems that previously required bespoke analysis tools to solve individually. LLMs lower the barrier to entry for writing these tools, allowing CRS builders to create components that are robust to different software versions, build tools, and environments while often remaining as capable as more fragile, complex analyses. One such analysis task is to demonstrate the proof of a vulnerability (PoV) in the form of an input that triggers the bug (2). Example 2 showcases one such PoV input for an exemplar bug from the Jenkins exemplar program for the AIxCC. In order to reach the buggy code, a CRS must craft a structured message with several fields exactly matched to particular byte strings. The Value field, however, is hashed before it is compared to a desired byte string, making it effectively impossible for typical constraint-solving approaches, such as symbolic execution, to solve analytically, as this would amount to reversing an SHA256 hash. Yet, without additional training, a language model is able to generalize enough to attain the effect of a constraint solver without this limitation. To showcase this capability, we use a component of our example CRS. This tool gathers path constraints to a particular target location using static analysis and coalesces them into a prompt (Example 3). When querying the language model, out of 30 prompts, it synthesizes six correct and four nearly correct inputs. The nearly correct test inputs have an additional null terminator, but are otherwise an exact match. We also ran the greybox fuzzer Jazzer for 24 hours, providing it with a “dictionary” file that contains all string constants present in the source code of the SUT, including the Header and Value strings needed for triggering the bug. In none of the 20 trials was the fuzzer able to find the vulnerability.

Example 3. Prompting template and templated values.

Prompting Template

You are a penetration testing engineer tasked with testing a Jenkins application.

Now, we notice there is one or more possible vulnerabilities of {{Vulnerability Class}}.

Note that we have a very different definition of this type of vulnerability. Here is the definition:

{{Sanitizer Definition}}

Please ensure:

{% for c in Path Constraints %} {{c}} n {%endfor%}

Here are the dataflow slices of the program:

{% for s in Dataflow Slices %} {{s}} n {%endfor%}

This is a file that is related to the input data:

{{Fuzz-Harness Source Code}}

Generate a Python program that dumps the expected data to the file ‘input.bin’

Sample Templated Values
"Vulnerability Class": "OsCommandInjection",
"Sanitizer Definition": "for OSCommandInjection, APIs like `java.lang.
     ProcessBuilder' must contain a magic word named `jazze'
     rather than the actual command",
"Path Constraints": [
 "containsHeader(rqst.getHeaderNames(),"x-evil-backdoor") == true",
 "MessageDigest.isEqual(sha256, providedHash)) == true" ],
"Dataflow Slices": [
 "boolean isAllowed=jenkins().hasPermission(Jenkins.ADMINISTER);",
 "byte[] sha256 = DigestUtils.sha256("breakin the law");",
 "String backdoorValue = request.getHeader("x-evil-backdoor");",
 "byte[] providedHash = DigestUtils.sha256(backdoorValue);", ...

Flexibility across languages.  We manually translated the extracted constraints and harness in Example 3 from Java to Python, resulting in Example 4. We then passed these Python constraints to an LLM in the exact same prompting template with no modifications. In this scenario, the model was still able to solve the Python program constraints in 8/30 trials. This is remarkable, in that the model did not require any changes to solve these constraints at the source level in both languages.

Example 4. Python Constraints passed to an LLM in the template of Example 3 for the translated Jenkins exemplar.

Templated Values Translated to Python
"Vulnerability Class": "OsCommandInjection",
"Sanitizer Definition":
 "for OSCommandInjection, APIs like `subprocess.Popen' must contain
       a magic word named `jazze' rather than the actual command",
 "Path Constraints": [
   ""x-evil-backdoor" in request.headers",
   cryptography.constant_time.bytes_eq(sha256, providedHash)",
],
"Dataflow Slices": [
 "isAllowed=jenkins().hasPermission(Jenkins.ADMINISTER)",
 "sha256=cryptography.hashes.Hash(crypto.hashes.SHA256()).update("
       breakin the law").finalize()")",
 "backdoorValue = request.headers["x-evil-backdoor"]",
 "providedHash = cryptography.hashes.Hash(crypto.hashes.SHA256())
       .update(backdoorValue).finalize()")",
    ...
]

Opportunities for agentic AI.  AI agents go beyond basic in-context learning by enabling LLMs to iteratively plan, reason, and take actions, such as invoking program-analysis tools. As a result, they are often able to achieve complex, multi-step goals fully autonomously.

Agents can reasonably be used to instantiate any individual components of the CRS. Indeed, our example CRS uses the AutoCodeRover22,28 LLM agent to fix vulnerabilities (6). This agent uses code navigation and analysis tools to iteratively develop and refine a patch. AutoCodeRover has now been integrated in the widely used SonarQube static-analysis tool to automatically fix detected security bugs, showing its practicality.

Beyond individual analysis steps, the orchestration layer (7) is particularly well suited to AI agents; it involves numerous and repeated challenging planning decisions that would previously have been the purview of a human auditor. For example: If a vulnerability is found, should the CRS conduct additional static analysis of the crash location or should it instead allocate those resources to further dynamic analysis of the vulnerable execution? What information from these analyses should be passed as context to the patch generator? As agents have begun to show significant progress in many individual tasks such as remediation,28 a more general and comprehensive deployment of LLM agents as the CRS itself is a promising area for future research.

Challenges for LLMs in CRSs

Unsoundness.  A key strength of many static vulnerability scanners (1) is their soundness; they can sometimes guarantee a program to be free of particular vulnerabilities. An LLM cannot guarantee soundness alone,21 so we see determining how to recover these guarantees or increased assurance of correctness as a key challenge for LLM-based tools in a CRS.

Incompleteness.   As language models are statistical models at their core, they can also give false positive answers to analysis queries, amounting to an incomplete analysis. This may be mitigated in some situations where an efficient checking oracle exists;24 for example, in the input-generation (3) task discussed earlier (see “Generalizability and flexibility of LLMs”), the LLM can be called iteratively until the oracle is passed (i.e., the bug is triggered). Identifying these oracles will be an important area of research in CRSs.

Training and evaluation data.  Language models are highly dependent on the data used to train them. Because of their size, their corresponding training sets must also be extremely large to avoid over-parameteriation. Assembling large enough datasets for all components of the CRS workflow without introducing the risk of data leakage5 will be crucial to the development and evaluation of CRSs.

Related Work

Vulnerability repair.   Applications of LLMs that can be utilized in a CRS include code generation2 and bug repairs.6 Early results indicate that LLM-based repair tools tend to outperform traditional repair tools on established datasets.26 Similarly, LLMs have begun to be used for localizing the fault,11 with promising initial results. Agentic systems can automatically explore a project or invoke subtasks via repeated interactions with the LLMs.22,28

Vulnerability discovery.  LLMs have also shown their capability in generating test harnesses16 and unit tests.13 Furthermore, they have been effective in fuzzing network protocols by providing the fuzzer a grammar for mutating the input messages.18 In addition to dynamic analysis, LLMs are also beginning to see success in augmenting static analysis, particularly for bug discovery.15

Benchmarks.   SWEBench10 is a widely used benchmark containing natural language descriptions of GitHub issues to be resolved by LLMs. These issues are mostly bug fixes, but can span multiple files. Other benchmarks have even been developed to specifically evaluate LLM capabilities and risks.24 To date, available benchmarks still only evaluate CRS sub-components, rather than a complete workflow; we hope they can provide a starting point for a general CRS benchmark.

Threats to Validity

As CRSs are an emerging research area, our study was limited to the only dataset available for end-to-end CRS evaluation. As such, our results may not generalize beyond this dataset (see “Challenges for LLMs in CRSs”). We did not evaluate the repair components in our CRS against traditional program-repair research tools—a threat to the internal validity. However, we excluded these tools from the CRS due to their poor generality (see “Generalizability and flexibility of LLMs”); similar comparisons have been published in prior work.26

Perspectives

Innovative technologies often disrupt established sectors in unexpected ways with surprising speed. We believe LLMs represent such a change, not just in how we write code, but also in how we reason about it. Automating this reasoning will be critical in keeping up with our ever-growing, increasingly complex software infrastructure and its security.
