While we can already see concrete benefits in augmenting or replacing existing software analysis with LLMs, such as fuzzing, symbolic execution, or static analysis, many more possibilities remain. Broadly, we believe LLMs can complement gaps in existing analyses by grasping developer intent, generalizing to different programming constructs and environments, and providing agentic multi-step reasoning spanning numerous individual analyses. By focusing on these opportunities, researchers will be able to push the frontier of what is possible in an integrated cyber reasoning system.

Acknowledgments

We thank Cristian Cadar (Imperial), Van-Thuan Pham (Melbourne), Lin Tan and Xiangyu Zhang (Purdue), and their respective research groups for their collaboration. This research is partially supported by the National Research Foundation, Singapore, and Cyber Security Agency of Singapore under its National Cybersecurity R&D Programme (Fuzz Testing <NRF-NCR25-Fuzz-0001>) and by a Singapore Ministry of Education (MoE) Tier 3 research grant (Automated Program Repair <MOE-MOET32021-0001>). Any opinions, findings and conclusions, or recommendations expressed in this material are those of the author(s) and do not reflect the views of National Research Foundation, Singapore, MoE or Cyber Security Agency of Singapore.
item.dc:creator: Martin Mirchev
Abhik Roychoudhury

item.title: From Volunteerism to Duty: Reforming Peer Review with Tokens
item.dc:creator: Matteo Francia, Enrico Gallinucci, and Matteo Golfarelli
item.description: Presenting a compulsory model that turns peer review into a reciprocal obligation.
item.content:encoded:
Reviewing is a time-honored practice. Peer review, in its modern form, began to spread in the 18th and 19th centuries, becoming standard in the 20th century. Although today software supports reviewers in handling the process and performing automatic controls (e.g., plagiarism detection), the review process has remained fundamentally unchanged for centuries. But over the past decade, there has been a progressive lengthening of the review time for scientific articles. This problem was felt acutely during the COVID-19 pandemic, when the rapid circulation of pandemic-related findings was particularly critical.1 Today, it is estimated that the average review time for journals in computer science is about three months, exceeding one year in many cases. The result: a major slowdown in innovation and the circulation of ideas, as well as harm to authors, who see their scientific productivity reduced. And in a fast-moving world, the risk of obsolescence of ideas also increases. In addition, the frantic search for reviewers and the risk of delayed reviews greatly increase the workload of associate editors. A recent survey within the data-management community showed how the problems of high reviewer load and low review engagement are ongoing, highly relevant issues that cause significant concern to the community.7

Key Insights

  • The peer-review system is facing a critical imbalance: While paper submissions continue to grow, reviewer availability remains stagnant, resulting in longer decision times and increased editorial workloads.

  • Voluntary and reward-based initiatives have proven insufficient in resolving this issue.

  • We need to transform peer review from a voluntary activity into a reciprocal obligation.

  • Although the basic idea is simple, making it work and accepted by the academic community raises many challenges.

The problem arises from an exponential increase in submissions that is not counterbalanced by the availability of reviewers. This phenomenon seems to be related to emerging geographic areas; for example, for China, India, and Iran, the ratio between the number of submitted papers and reviews is more than three to one.9 Why the disparity? One answer is that while authoring is considered a central part of researchers’ work on which careers are often built, reviewing is done on a voluntary basis and is bound only by an ethical covenant between researchers. The problem is more severe in scientific journals than at international conferences. At the latter, the program committee and the author community overlap much more, so there is a common interest in completing reviews by the conference deadline.2

An orthogonal problem is how to ensure the quality of reviews—and, in turn, the quality of the paper, which partially depends on the work of the researchers who read and evaluate it, requesting additions and explanations, or reject it. Currently, there is no mechanism for certifying the quality of reviewers,8 and the final judgment is left to the editor’s assessment.

Previous attempts to curb the problem have been based on the logic of rewarding and reducing the review effort:

  • ReviewerCredits4 is a start-up that offers a reward (even monetary) to researchers who rely on the platform. In exchange for their willingness to review texts, researchers receive virtual credits and a certification index that measures their activity so that not only what they publish, but also what they are asked to evaluate, counts in their resume. The platform also offers a service to publishers: Journals that subscribe to ReviewerCredits in search of reviewers have guaranteed credibility and transparency, so it becomes easier for reviewers to assess the quality of journals and for the latter to find accredited reviewers. The ReviewerCredits business model is based on fees paid by publishers.

  • Peerage of Science6 is a collaborative peer-review platform designed to improve the quality and efficiency of the scientific review process. Authors submit their manuscripts, which are then evaluated by volunteer reviewers based on quality and scientific rigor. The review process is open and interactive, allowing reviewers to comment on each other’s assessments to refine the overall evaluation. Once the peer review is completed, authors can submit the revised manuscript to one of the partner journals, increasing the chances of acceptance thanks to an already established assessment. The system encourages reviewer participation through a scoring mechanism based on the quality of their evaluations.

  • Publons9 was a platform that allowed researchers to track, verify, and gain recognition for their peer-review and academic editing work. Users could record the reviews they had conducted for scientific journals, building a public profile that showcased their contributions to the scientific community. Publons collaborated with publishers to verify reviews without disclosing their content, ensuring the confidentiality of the process. In addition, it provided statistics and useful metrics to monitor the impact of one’s reviewing activity. In 2019, Publons was integrated into Web of Science, becoming part of the academic reviewer recognition system managed by Clarivate.

The need for an adequate reward system for reviewers is widely recognized.5,11,12 However, in the increasingly fast-paced and competitive world of research, there is no certainty that awards (even if prestigious) will attract a sufficient number of reviewers. As emphasized by our survey, none of those interviewed consider these approaches truly useful.3 In fact, recent studies reveal that awards might actually have a (counterintuitive) discouraging effect,13 as they make awarded reviewers aware that their efforts are greater than the average, while the awards received are perceived as inadequate. Despite the attempts made so far, it is apparent that voluntary and reward-based approaches have failed to prove their effectiveness.

For these reasons, we propose a compulsory approach, based on tokens, that relies on the following elementary mechanism:

  • Each group of authors, in order to submit a paper, must provide a number of tokens equal to the number of reviews required by the journal.

  • For each review made, the reviewer receives one token from the journal.

The authors must also serve as reviewers, and each submission generates an equal number of reviews; this ensures that the system is in balance. Furthermore, as we argue in the next sections, the adoption of a token-based approach brings with it a series of positive consequences. We emphasize that the token-based approach is designed to support—not replace—the voluntary-based review that we believe is a natural task for a researcher. Apart from the ethical motivation, reviewing is essential for keeping up to date and finding new research ideas. And if the reviews are distributed evenly, reviewers will be able to choose only those that are most interesting to them, which may increase rather than reduce the spirit of volunteerism.

Finally, the token-based mechanism is designed as a security system to solve the ethical problem of non-collaborative and lazy researchers. The use of rules and laws to correct unethical behavior is often necessary when civic education alone proves insufficient. Examples range from smoking bans in public spaces to strict penalties for plagiarism in academia.

In the remainder of this article, we discuss our proposal in detail by using statistics and simulations to show how the problems we have identified are addressed by the system. Statistics are calculated by integrating DBLP data (with 2.85 million authors and more than 3 million papers published since 1936, excluding publications on arXiv) with Scimago information (with almost 2,000 journals) and statistics manually retrieved from the official websites of more than 700 journals.

System Architecture and Outcomes

The functional system architecture is shown in Figure 1. The Token Management System (TMS) interacts with the Publisher Management Systems (PMS) (e.g., ACM Editorial Manager or IEEE Author Portal) to implement the following process.

Authors submitting a paper must have a number of tokens equal to the number of reviews required by the journal (typically two or three). The PMS contacts the TMS, which verifies the token availability and subtracts them from the authors’ availability. Submitted papers are assigned to editors, who start the review process. Reviewers register on the TMS for specific subjects and set their availability to accept reviews. Given the paper’s subject, the editors invite available reviewers, though the reviewer still has the option of accepting or declining the invitation. When the reviewer submits a review to the PMS, the editor verifies the review quality and communicates to the TMS the assignment of a token to the author. In the case of a late or low-quality review, the editor may decide to assign only a fraction of a token.

To prevent fraud and rule exchanges, tokens can be implemented as a digital entity, representing a fungible unit of value. Tokens can be transferred through cryptographic protocols and governed by rules running in the TMS. Authors and reviewers must be identified using a universal identifier such as ORCID. Note that the mechanism ensures authors’ and reviewers’ privacy, since all information linking an author or a reviewer to a paper remains in the PMS. The TMS also enables the calculation of statistics on the activity and quality of reviewers, which can be useful to both editors and reviewers themselves to certify their work.

Implementation.  A simulator for the submission-review process has been implemented in Python, using the agent-based modeling library Mesa.a An in-depth description of the simulation model and the source code are available at the Business Intelligence Group website,3 enabling interested readers to run the simulator with different parameters.

The configuration of the simulation discussed here is based on the statistics for 2024, extracted from DBLP, Scimago, and journal websites (e.g., number of authors per paper, journal acceptance rate, average review time). The model simulates a community of 135,972 groups of around four authors. Each group of authors submits an average of nine papers per year and at the average acceptance rate of journals (19%). Each group of authors also acts as a reviewer. Different groups of authors give widely disparate availabilities for voluntary reviews, from four to 34 reviews per year (19 on average). We assume that eager authors have a lower review time (10 days on average, with SD of 2) than volunteer reviewers (81 days on average, with SD of 37). The limit to voluntary reviews can be exceeded only if the reviewer/author needs tokens.

The simulation was run for 2,190 iterations, with each iteration representing a day (totaling six years). The first three years simulate the peer-review system in current conditions (submissions are free and reviews are purely voluntary); then, the token-based mechanism is activated (at Day 0) and the simulation runs for three more years. The simulator models the TMS and the PMSs as a single central entity (where submitted papers are collected and sent for reviewing) and groups of authors as separate autonomous agents. Each agent has a status (either lazy or eager, effective only when the token-based system is active) and two queues: the submission queue  (papers ready to be submitted to a journal) and the reviewing queue (papers to be reviewed); papers in the reviewing queue are associated with a reviewing date, chosen by the agent. In every iteration, each agent performs the following actions:

  • Add a new paper to the submission queue (with probability 2.3%, so as to match the estimated total amount of papers submitted annually) and pick a journal to submit to (with 73% probability of picking a journal with two reviews needed, three otherwise).

  • Review papers in the reviewing queue if the reviewing date corresponds to the current iteration. If the token-based system is active, the agent earns one token.

  • Submit papers in the submission queue. If the token-based system is active, submission is possible only with enough tokens available (which are then spent). In the absence of enough tokens, the agent can decide to anticipate the reviewing date of papers in the reviewing queue (only in the amount needed to get the required tokens); if the reviewing queue is empty or insufficient to grant the needed tokens, the agent becomes eager.

Then, the central entity representing the TMS and the PMSs carries out the following operations for every submitted paper:

  • If the paper has been submitted over the last four iterations, it is kept on hold.

  • An invite is sent to a random agent for every review needed by the paper; if the token-based system is active, priority is given to eager agents. Agents answer immediately based on their status: A lazy agent accepts with 20% probability (based on survey outcomes) and only if the yearly availability for voluntary reviews has not been met over the past 365 iterations; an eager agent always accepts and possibly goes back to a lazy status (if its reviewing queue satisfies its needs for tokens).

  • If all needed reviews have been assigned, the paper is moved to a waiting queue (from which it is removed upon collection of all reviews).

Results.  The simulation outcomes (averaged over five runs) are reported in Figure 2, and include the following:

  • The activation of the token-based mechanism at Day 0 results in a strong reduction of the editorial queue due to authors who are eager to review papers to earn the tokens needed for submitting their own papers (blue line in Figure 2a).

  • Given the availability of eager reviewers, the number of invitations done by associate editors to assign reviews is also halved (green line in Figure 2a). This results in a further reduction in the submission-to-first-decision (STFD) time, since each submission takes four days in our simulation (red line in Figure 2a).

  • The matrix in Figure 2b shows the final distribution of the researchers based on the maximum number of voluntary reviews they initially foresaw (x-axis) and the number of reviews actually done (y-axis); for instance, the top-left cell contains researchers who volunteered for only two reviews but actually did nine, because they needed many tokens to make multiple submissions. Remarkably, cells above the diagonal of the heatmap are attributable to the token-based approach, highlighting lazy researchers who were unwilling to meaningfully contribute to the peer-review process, despite adding to its burden with their own submissions.

Challenge Analysis

In the following sections, we list risks and opportunities related to the system and how they can be addressed.

Token exchange and circulation.  Authors publish in different journals and potentially on different subjects; thus, a token could be earned by reviewing a paper from a certain publisher/journal/area and spent on a submission to a different journal/publisher/area. This criterion provides maximum flexibility for the authors to submit a paper and ensures a rapid return on the reviewing effort. Nonetheless, it opens the door to imbalances and opportunistic behaviors: An author/reviewer might try to acquire tokens in journals with a low level of complexity. This would make the number of reviews insufficient for journals with a high level of complexity. The same problem could arise for narrow research areas. To ensure a stronger balance, barriers can be placed on token usability by requiring that a token be spent in the same area, or for the same level of complexity, as the journal from which it was acquired. Managing barriers between areas requires flexibility, and a margin of discretion may be given to the associate editor in evaluating the token’s usability. A softer approach could be to assign greater value to reviews based on the journal complexity. The level of complexity of a journal can be determined by metrics such as its impact factor or the quartile it belongs to.

Enforcing review quality and timeliness.  The token-based model can also be used to reward the punctuality and quality of reviews. In fact, it is possible to refine the basic mechanism by imposing a rule that a token is granted only if the review is delivered on time and respects a predefined level of quality. Late or low-quality reviews can progressively generate a fraction of tokens. For example, the value of the token can be reduced by a fraction (e.g., 0.1) for each week of delay, and even set to negative for undelivered reviews.

The quality of a review can be assessed by the editor or by peer evaluation. It is unlikely that standardized, uniform criteria will be adopted across all disciplines, since the concept of quality can vary depending on the field and type of journal. For example, Sizo et al.10 have surveyed more than 100 metrics. Defining these metrics in detail is beyond the scope of this article, but it is important to underline that our approach allows for managing them. We also need to consider that punitive rewards are not well received by the community.7

While current voluntary reviews necessarily require longer review times, it is conceivable that they would shorten significantly in a token-based approach, both because it is in the reviewers’ interest to complete the review as soon as possible to have the token available, and because a delay may reduce the value of the token.

Token wear and reissuance.  The availability of reviews to be assigned is a necessary condition for not disrupting the flow of submissions. Under a constant flow of submissions, the “liquidity” of the system is guaranteed: Each submission consumes the same number of tokens previously produced through reviews. However, there are some scenarios in which the number of tokens consumed is greater than the number of tokens produced. Over time, this could become problematic, as authors may be unable to submit work due to having no way to earn the required tokens, given the lack of available reviews. This may arise from late reviews gaining only a fraction of tokens, authors quitting or retiring from research and leaving their tokens unused, or volunteer reviewers acquiring more tokens than they actually consume. The problem can be easily overcome by monitoring the quantity of tokens/reviews available and by implementing ad-hoc reissuance policies when necessary—that is, granting more tokens than are consumed by a submission, equivalent to increasing the number of reviews available. Editors can also exploit this mechanism to support the reviewing process; for instance, reviews of complex papers (or papers on less-attractive subjects) could be incentivized by issuing a higher number of tokens per review. Ensuring a sufficient level of liquidity is important to prevent further unethical behaviors, such as fake coauthorship, where the only contribution made by a coauthor is tokens. We believe that this risk becomes real only in the event of a severe token shortage.

Cold start.  A submission can take place only after the necessary tokens have been acquired, that is, only after the author has delivered the necessary reviews. This is a problem at system start-up because requiring reviews in advance would impose a delay on all submissions. It must also be considered that, at start-up, the system inherits the previous imbalance; that is, there are a large number of active submissions that lack reviews. Solutions to this problem include:

  • Each publisher can initially provide tokens to authors in a fixed number, or based on reviews made in the previous year.

  • In the start-up phase, reviews can be delivered within the paper submission period. This avoids delaying submission and ties publication to the performance of the review activity.

In our simulations, we granted three tokens to all the authors when the token-based approach was activated at Day 0. This ensured a smooth transition, since publishing was not stalled. We emphasize that the three-token welcome bonus mitigates the start-up phase, but the introduction of a reciprocity solution requires researchers to change their perspective: In order to publish, they must also review. Once this mental shift has been made, variations in productivity during one’s career or due to seasonality will not create problems: If one is more or less productive, one will also have to do more or fewer reviews.

Rookie authors.  Due to limited experience and skills, new authors may not have the opportunity to access the journal review process and accumulate tokens. This is only a problem in the case where the article is single-authored or all authors are rookies. Statistics indicate this occurrence is very rare—only 2.3% of papers in the DBLP repository in the past 10 years. In other cases, the remaining authors can provide the necessary tokens. To support submissions from rookie authors, it is necessary to provide waivers that allow submission and generate an honor debt (i.e., a negative number of tokens for the author) that must be repaid by performing reviews in subsequent years. Alternatively, a non-coauthor researcher can pay the necessary tokens, playing the role of mentor to the rookie.

Complexities in transition.  The main complexities in deploying the system described here are organizational and social in nature. From a technological point of view, the functional architecture proposed in Figure 1 is not particularly complex and is similar to many cloud-based applications. To ensure the reliability of the entire system, it is important that it can be managed by an independent, nonprofit entity (as in the case of ORCID). Potential points of attention include the definition of the communication interfaces between the TMS and the various PMSs, as well as the evaluation of how to make the TMS redundant in order to avoid it becoming a single point of failure.

It is much more complex to guide the transition and encourage adoption by journal publishers and the scientific community. We draw attention to the following focus points:

  • Selecting the experimentation area: It is wise to initially apply the approach to a limited disciplinary area, both to check for any side effects when the system is actually in use and to simplify the choice of the alternatives and thresholds discussed in the previous step. For example, reasoning on a single discipline will ease the selection of the review-quality standard. As already mentioned, some effects of our proposal are difficult to assess in advance and require a field-testing phase. In principle, it would be possible to apply the system to a single journal or a single publisher rather than a complete disciplinary area, but it is difficult to forecast whether authors would be attracted by the reduced review time or discouraged by the compulsory review task.

  • Setting the process details and thresholds: The approach proposed so far still requires choosing the best solution among the alternatives for mitigating the various risks and defining the detailed parameters (e.g., quality metrics and thresholds, token decay rules). At this stage, in order to overcome aversion to change and mistrust, it is essential to involve publishers and associate editors, since the “best choice” is ultimately the one that secures the broadest consensus.

Authors are required to change their mindset by reorganizing their way of working, anticipating the need for tokens by acquiring them in advance. Only researchers with a negative review-submission balance will experience an extra workload, while virtuous researchers and associate editors should see their effort reduced after an initial adjustment phase.

Discussion

The key point of our proposal is shifting from a voluntary or reward-based approach to a compulsory model for peer review. The concept of peer review should no longer be understood solely as “among peers of similar expertise” but also as a system based on “reciprocity.” It is important to note that the proposed system does not prevent researchers from voluntarily reviewing papers without necessarily earning tokens.

The in-depth discussion, statistical analysis, and simulations presented in this study demonstrate that the proposed system effectively balances the review-to-submission ratio. It allows for the evaluation and appreciation of review quality, and significantly reduces review times, ultimately shortening the publication process.

To encourage discussion, we close with the results of a survey submitted to a subset of chief and associate editors of computer science journals, chosen by varying subject areas, publishers, and quality of the journals. We collected 30 responses out of 100 invitations.3 The main outcomes include the following:

  • The vast majority of responses confirm the existence of the problem and the need to find a solution. The seriousness of the problem of delay in the review process in peer-reviewed journals due to the lack of available reviewers was rated 4 or 5 (on a scale from 1 to 5) by 90% of respondents, and was noted as worsening over the past few years by 97% of respondents. Their experience with the length of the submission-to-first-decision process is very diverse (from two to 30 months), with an average of 8.4 months (SD 6.4); notably, this is worse than the official metrics published by journals, which typically indicate three months on average.

  • The most recurring concern (45%) is the risk of a decrease in review quality (as some reviewers would be forced to do them to earn tokens). We emphasize that our approach foresees a mechanism for rewarding review quality through the possibility of providing portions of tokens if the review does not meet quality standards.

  • Twenty-four percent of respondents indicated that junior researchers may be disadvantaged by the system (mainly due to a lack of experience in reviewing) and recommend allowing some flexibility in the token mechanism. However, DBLP statistics indicate that junior researchers most often collaborate with seniors, who have the skills and experience to review other papers and earn the needed tokens.

  • Only 17% are familiar with Publons and related initiatives; among them, none of the respondents perceive them as being able to tackle the problem.

We hope this article sparks a broader discussion within the scientific community, leading to a shift away from the current review-submission imbalance, which poses a significant barrier to scientific production and, consequently, to the rapid dissemination of innovations.

Acknowledgments

We would like to thank our colleagues who encouraged us to pursue this research, convinced of its relevance and potential impact, and the associate editors who participated in the survey by providing their experience and opinions.
item.dc:creator: Enrico Gallinucci
Matteo Golfarelli

item.title: Empowerment over Automation in AI Summarization of Online Reviews
item.dc:creator: Nima Kordzadeh
item.description: AI-generated review summaries must enable awareness, comparison, and control so that users remain active interpreters rather than silent recipients of algorithmic consensus.
item.content:encoded:
Imagine you are preparing to book a hotel for a long-awaited family trip. Instead of scrolling through hundreds of mixed reviews, the site presents a single AI-generated summary: “Guests loved the central location and friendly staff, but many noted noise at night and outdated bathrooms.” In less than 10 seconds, you feel ready to decide.

This convenience is no longer hypothetical. Amazon now places AI-generated review highlights at the top of product pages, distilling thousands of comments into a few key takeaways, such as ease of use, comfort, or quality, so shoppers can grasp the consensus at a glance. TripAdvisor and Yelp do the same, condensing community sentiment into compact snapshots. In this way, AI has become the new interpreter of collective voice, deciding what matters most, what gets overlooked, and how consensus is framed.1,6

Yet summarization is never neutral. By choosing which signals to highlight, which to omit, and how to frame the message, algorithms shape perception and guide choice. This is consequential: the way opinions are summarized can influence how users interpret information, the confidence they place in their choices, and the fairness and credibility of the broader review ecosystem. Accordingly, the central question is not whether AI-generated summaries are useful, as they clearly are, but whether they empower users to make informed judgments or nudge them into passive acceptance. Addressing this dilemma, this Opinion column takes the following position: AI-generated review summaries are inevitable, but they must be designed for human agency—enabling awareness, comparison, and control—so users remain active interpreters rather than silent recipients of algorithmic consensus.

I argue that only when users can see how a summary is formed, explore multiple perspectives, and interact with underlying evidence can trust become informed and accountability meaningful. To build this case, this Opinion column first examines the good, the bad, and the ugly of AI-mediated summarization across three stakeholders—users, brands, and platforms—to show how tools built for convenience may unintentionally limit awareness, reshape agency, and weaken trust. It then advances a design philosophy centered on empowerment over automation and proposes three practical principles that transform summarization into a more transparent, participatory, and trustworthy process.

The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly of AI Summaries

The effects of AI-generated review summaries are multifaceted, influencing not only how users interpret and act on information but also how brands are represented and how platforms build trust. These summaries can simplify complexity and help users make sense of overwhelming feedback, while offering businesses quick insight into public sentiment and helping platforms enhance usability. Yet the same mechanisms that deliver convenience can also narrow understanding and distort perception. Accordingly, examining their benefits, pitfalls, and risks across stakeholders clarifies how summarization can either support or suppress user agency, reshape brand reputation, and strengthen or weaken platform credibility, revealing the conditions under which convenience empowers or constrains human judgment.

The good.  At their best, AI-generated summaries help users cut through noise and make sense of overwhelming information. By surfacing recurring themes, such as durability, customer service, or location, and presenting key trade-offs, they reduce cognitive overload and support focused comparison. Summaries that balance breadth and clarity allow users to interpret information on their own terms, rather than surrendering to information fatigue. They can be particularly helpful for individuals with limited time, lower digital literacy, or language barriers, who might otherwise struggle to navigate large volumes of text. In these cases, automation complements human judgment, providing clarity without displacing autonomy.

When well implemented, such summaries also help brands spotlight genuine strengths and gain timely insights into customer perceptions, while enabling platforms to deliver smoother, more efficient user experiences that foster trust.

The bad.  Empowerment weakens when summarization oversimplifies or obscures. Condensing hundreds of diverse opinions into a few lines inevitably filters nuance. If algorithms prioritize popularity or sentiment over diversity and context, they produce partial narratives that may not reflect users’ needs. For example, a hotel’s review summary might highlight great nightlife while overlooking recurring noise complaints that matter to families. Cognitive biases such as anchoring or confirmation bias can further narrow interpretation, making users overly reliant on highlighted cues.2 As clarity gives way to reductionism, users’ interpretive power shrinks, and decisions feel convenient but less informed, signaling a subtle shift from autonomy to algorithmic dependence.

These shortcomings ripple outward: brands may find their reputations misrepresented through unbalanced portrayals, and platforms risk diminished credibility when users perceive bias or superficiality in the summaries they provide.

The ugly.  The most serious threat arises when summarization distorts reality, presenting skewed or selectively positive accounts. Users may be steered toward conclusions that conflict with the underlying evidence, such as exaggerated praise for cleanliness while omitting consistent reports of poor hygiene. Over time, such manipulation corrodes trust and undermines voluntary judgment, leaving users unsure whether they are being informed or influenced. When summarization shifts from assistance to agenda-setting, autonomy collapses, and the very tools meant to empower begin to constrain.

Distorted narratives can damage brand reputations and draw scrutiny toward platforms perceived as amplifying bias or privileging certain voices. Such perceptions undermine fairness and put long-term legitimacy at risk.

The accompanying table provides a stakeholder-centric overview of the good, the bad, and the ugly of AI summaries, underscoring how quickly efficiency gains can turn into distortion and trust erosion.

Looking Ahead: Designing for Empowerment

If AI-generated review summaries are here to stay, their value should be judged not only by accuracy or efficiency but by how they shape human agency. The question is no longer whether automation can summarize effectively; what matters more is whether it amplifies or suppresses users’ ability to think critically, compare alternatives, and make choices aligned with their goals.

Prior research has shown that when AI systems simplify or abstract complex information to guide human decision-making, they can unintentionally reinforce user biases or narrow attention rather than expanding understanding.2,4 In the long run, platforms that privilege speed over comprehension may win clicks but lose credibility. The future of AI summarization therefore depends on designing for empowerment.

True empowerment means users remain aware, informed, and in control—able to interpret summaries rather than absorb them uncritically. Achieving this requires a deliberate shift from black-box automation to transparent, participatory systems. Accordingly, I propose a design philosophy grounded in four principles: transparency, plurality, user control, and accountability, as depicted in the accompanying figure.

Transparency: Turning hidden logic into visible process.  Transparency is the foundation of agency. When users understand how a summary is built—what data it draws from, what rules guide inclusion, and what limitations exist—they can interpret it critically rather than accept it blindly.

A simple disclosure such as “This summary was generated from 1,250 verified reviews, using AI to identify recurring themes in cleanliness, service, and value” sets expectations and communicates scope. Even brief notes about date range or selection criteria help users grasp what the summary can and cannot say.

Beyond static labels, platforms should offer interactive transparency: features like “trace this insight” links that reveal sample supporting reviews or confidence scores for each insight. These lightweight cues turn summarization into a visible reasoning process, allowing users to inspect, verify, and question rather than merely consume. Such openness reshapes cognition, as explanations and traceability foster calibrated trust and reduce automation bias.5

Transparency also signals platform integrity. By showing their work, platforms shift from acting as silent arbiters to becoming partners in reasoning, acknowledging uncertainty, highlighting trade-offs, and empowering users to weigh evidence themselves. In this way, transparency becomes a social contract: it invites trust through openness, not authority, and lays the groundwork for accountability across all stakeholders.

Plurality: Replacing singular narratives with diverse perspectives.  Plurality means representing multiple viewpoints within AI-generated summaries. It ensures that many voices remain visible in the translation from reviews to summaries. A single “consensus” summary, while convenient, can flatten legitimate disagreement, masking diversity in preferences, experiences, or priorities. Users rarely share identical needs; families, solo travelers, and business guests may value different aspects of the same hotel. By presenting multiple perspectives, summaries help users locate themselves within the spectrum of opinion; single-voice summaries, in contrast, steer them toward a one-size-fits-all conclusion.

Designing for plurality can take many forms: side-by-side summaries of most praised and most criticized aspects; segmented insights for distinct user groups; or visual indicators of sentiment distribution across themes. These features invite users to compare and interpret rather than absorb a single narrative. By revealing the range of sentiment instead of collapsing it into an average, plurality restores the context and contrast necessary for judgment.

Plurality also acts as a cognitive safeguard. Exposure to diverse perspectives helps counteract anchoring and confirmation biases, which can arise when users fixate on the first or most salient message. When multiple viewpoints are visible, users are encouraged to weigh competing signals and form independent conclusions.

Most importantly, plurality repositions AI from final judge to conversation starter—a tool that frames options, not one that dictates them. By making disagreement visible and comparison natural, plural summaries reinforce users’ interpretive role and preserve agency within complexity.

User control: Turning summaries into interactive tools.  Agency strengthens when users can shape what they see. Static summaries often imply a single interpretation, but interactive designs allow individuals to explore information according to their needs. Interactive interfaces such as sliders to adjust time windows (for example, “last six months”), filters to focus on specific reviewer groups (for example, “families only”), or toggles to emphasize certain aspects (for example, cleanliness versus location) give users room to experiment with perspective and tailor summaries to their needs. These controls transform summarization into a collaborative process. By engaging in guided exploration, users move from passive reception to active interpretation, seeing how different settings influence outcomes. In this way, the experience becomes more reflective and less prescriptive, encouraging thoughtful comparison.

Meaningful control also supports cognitive calibration. When people can trace how each adjustment alters emphasis, they become more aware of trade-offs and potential bias. This awareness reduces overreliance on default views and nurtures a sense of ownership in the decision process. In practical terms, adjustable summaries can help a traveler focus on recent comments during peak season or a shopper prioritize durability over appearance.

Effective user control balances simplicity with flexibility. Too many levers can overwhelm, yet a few intuitive options, including clear filters, visible rationale, and reversible choices, can reinforce autonomy without adding friction. Well-designed interactivity signals that the platform values interpretation as much as efficiency, aligning technology with the user’s judgment.

Accountability: Embedding oversight into the system.  Transparency, plurality, and user control are only effective when paired with genuine accountability. Without mechanisms to monitor and correct distortions, even well-designed summaries can drift toward bias, omission, or manipulation. Accountability ensures that platforms remain answerable for how their algorithms represent collective opinion and influence user choices.

Practical accountability begins with regular audits that evaluate the balance and accuracy of summaries across products, languages, and user groups. These audits should test whether summaries reflect the underlying data fairly, avoid privileging certain sentiments, and reveal consistent weaknesses in model behavior. Publicly reporting the results of such evaluations, even in brief form, reinforces credibility and signals an institutional commitment to fairness.

Platforms should also provide accessible feedback channels for users and businesses to contest misleading summaries or highlight overlooked perspectives. Responsive correction builds trust and transforms accountability from a static compliance exercise into a living dialogue with stakeholders.

In high-impact domains, independent oversight can strengthen legitimacy. External reviewers or industry consortia can establish shared benchmarks for representativeness and bias mitigation. Clear disclosure standards, such as stating data coverage, model updates, or known limitations, help regulators and the public evaluate integrity without stifling innovation.

Embedding accountability into design and governance transforms AI summaries into trustworthy mediators of collective voice. As such, responsibility becomes central to systems that seek to inform users and support fair, transparent decision making.

From automation to agency.  When transparency, plurality, user control, and accountability operate together, AI-generated summaries evolve from passive simplifiers into active facilitators of meaningful decision making. In such systems, users can trace the formation of insights, compare perspectives side by side, and adjust what they see to align with their goals, cultivating awareness, critical reflection, and confidence in outcomes. Platforms signal openness and responsibility, while brands engage in ecosystems where reputation reflects authentic collective experience.

Information curation determines what becomes visible and how meaning takes shape. The aim is to align automation with human reasoning so that efficiency enhances autonomy instead of displacing it. This balance echoes the broader automation–augmentation paradox noted in organizational research, which emphasizes that the value of AI emerges not from replacing human judgment but from augmenting it through complementary design choices.3

Conclusion

AI-generated review summaries sit at the crossroads of convenience and control. They promise clarity yet carry the risk of distortion, offering speed that can shape understanding in subtle ways. Their significance lies in how they influence human agency, as every design choice—what to include, omit, or emphasize—affects how people interpret, compare, and decide. The task now is to build systems that expand awareness and invite reflection.

Summaries should help users see patterns without hiding nuance, encourage judgment rather than replace it, and foster awareness that strengthens trust and promotes appropriate reliance on AI as a partner in reasoning. Ultimately, the true measure of progress lies in how AI deepens human understanding and supports thoughtful judgment. Tools built on respect for human agency can expand understanding and give voice to the full richness of collective judgment. Such design choices move AI closer to responsible and accountable practice.

item.title: In Memoriam: Michael O. Rabin
item.dc:creator: Eugene H. Spafford and Simson Garfinkel
item.description: ACM Turing Award laureate Michael Rabin's professional contributions touch nearly every aspect of computing.
item.content:encoded:
“Theoretical ideas have a real-life impact,” said Michael Oser Rabin in a 2009 interview,14 and he spent nearly seven decades proving it. He died on April 14, 2026, in Jerusalem, Israel, at the age of 94. An ACM A.M. Turing Award Laureate, his contributions touch nearly every aspect of computing.

Michael Rabin was born September 1, 1931 in Breslau, Germany (now Wrocław, Poland). He fled with his family to Mandatory Palestine in September 1935, narrowly escaping the enactment of the Nuremberg Laws by the Nazi regime. His family settled in the port city of Haifa.

Intensely interested in mathematics from an early age, Rabin told of getting kicked out of class when he was 11 years old and encountering two ninth graders trying to solve a geometry problem. Even though he had never studied geometry, he could solve it. “The fact that you can, by pure thought, prove statements about the real world of lines, circles, and distances impressed me so deeply that I decided I wanted to study mathematics.”14

Rabin was influenced in high school by the mathematician Elisha Netanyahu—uncle of Israel’s future prime minister—who ran a weekly mathematics circle and lent Rabin advanced texts in number theory, algebra, and analysis.

Israel’s War of Independence broke out in 1948, and Rabin, along with everyone else in his high school class, was drafted into the army. He was discharged in September 1949 to study with Abraham Fraenkel at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem, which had lost its Mount Scopus campus the year before and was operating out of temporary, makeshift buildings throughout West Jerusalem.

At Hebrew University, Rabin read Stephen Cole Kleene’s Introduction to Metamathematics and the chapter on Alan Turing’s work on Computable Numbers. Rabin said, “Upon reading papers on computability and witnessing the advent of computers, I realized that this new technology would give rise to a new kind of mathematics explaining and advancing the new field of computing. I decided that I want to participate in and contribute to this development.” He earned his M. Sc. in 1953.

Rabin left Israel in 1954 to study with Alonzo Church at Princeton University. He received his Ph.D. in June 1957, then spent the summer of 1957 with his Princeton classmate Dana S. Scott at the IBM Summer Research Program in Westchester, NY. It was there the two wrote the first draft of their paper “Finite Automata and Their Decision Problems,”13 which formalized the concept of nondeterministic finite automata and showed they were computationally equivalent to deterministic machines. They also showed that multi-tape Turing machines were equivalent to single-tape machines. For this work, the pair was awarded the 11th Turing Award in 1976. The citation read: “Along with Dana S. Scott, for their joint paper ‘Finite Automata and Their Decision Problem,’ which introduced the idea of nondeterministic machines, which has proved to be an enormously valuable concept. Their (Scott & Rabin) classic paper has been a continuous source of inspiration for subsequent work in this field.”1

Before leaving Princeton, Kurt Gödel invited Rabin to the Institute for Advanced Study, where the two met weekly through 1958. Rabin later described those sessions as “incredibly illuminating.” Rabin returned to IBM’s summer research program and shifted his focus from the question of “what is computable?” to the question of how costly computation might be. He proved that for every computable function, there exists another function that is provably more difficult to compute, thus establishing a genuine hierarchy of computational difficulty independent of hardware or programming language. A key realization was that complexity is “inherent in the task.” Many point to Rabin’s 1960 publication9 as the birth of what is now called complexity theory.

In 1969, Rabin solved what he characterized as “the most difficult research I have ever done”: the monadic second-order theory of two successor functions (S2S).8 The proof required inventing automata that operate on infinite binary trees rather than strings. The result made decidable an entire family of logical theories, including the temporal logics that underlie modern program verification.

Rabin returned to Hebrew University in 1958 as a faculty member; he was rapidly promoted in recognition of his impact in what was becoming the field of computer science. He was named chair of the Institute of Mathematics in 1964. With Eli Shamir, he co-founded the university’s department of computer science in 1970, becoming its first chair, overcoming faculty skepticism about the legitimacy of the new field. In 1972, at the age of 41, he was elected Hebrew University’s Rector, the university’s chief academic officer, and served for three years—a period that also included the Yom Kippur War of 1973. Rabin visited each wounded student from any school in the hospital and admitted each unconditionally. He later said simply there were times when compassion mattered more than the rules.7

In 1975, Rabin took a sabbatical year at MIT’s Laboratory for Computer Science. There, he met Gary L. Miller, whose Ph.D. thesis explored a new approach for proving whether a number is prime or composite without factoring. The approach relied on finding numbers, which Rabin would later call “witnesses,” that proved a candidate number is composite, but the approach also relied on the Riemann hypothesis being true, which bothered Rabin.

Rabin realized witness numbers were far more common than Miller had assumed. In so doing, he was able to turn Miller’s test from a slow, deterministic test that depended on the extended Riemann hypothesis into a fast, probabilistic test that could determine primality with arbitrarily high probability. The new approach, now called the Miller-Rabin Primality Test, was published in 1980.12 That test became the basis for picking prime numbers for use with the RSA public key cryptography algorithm.

In that same period, Rabin turned to cryptography. In his 1979 paper on the Rabin signature algorithm, building on the same number-theoretic foundations, he established the first asymmetric cryptosystem whose security was provably equivalent to the hardness of integer factorization—something RSA does not provide.10 Rabin later recalled that he also introduced the distinction between what are now called the Monte Carlo and Las Vegas algorithms, a distinction still used in the field. He developed oblivious transfer in 1981.11 In 1987, Rabin and Richard M. Karp co-developed the Rabin-Karp rolling-hash string-search algorithm,2 widely used in plagiarism detection and intrusion detection systems.

Rabin joined the faculty of Harvard University as Gordon McKay Professor of Computer Science in 1981. He transitioned to the Thomas J. Watson Sr. Professorship of Computer Science in 1983, a position he held until 2012, when he became a research professor. Yet even while at Harvard, Rabin continued to return to Hebrew University regularly. He also held visiting appointments at Yale University, the Weizmann Institute, the Technion, the University of California Berkeley, MIT, the University of Paris, Caltech, the Courant Institute, ETH Zürich, Columbia University, and King’s College London; he held fellowships at Merton College Oxford and Gonville and Caius College Cambridge. From 1980 to 1994, he served on the IBM Science Advisory Committee. In the spring of 2009, he became a Visiting Researcher at Google.

In addition to the Turing Award, Rabin received the Israel Prize in Computer Science in 1995, the ACM Paris Kanellakis Theory and Practice Award in 2004 (shared with Gary Miller, Robert Solovay, and Volker Strassen for the primality testing work), the IACR Fellow designation in 2009, the Dan David Prize in Computing and Communications in 2010, and the Edsger W. Dijkstra Prize in Distributed Computing in 2015. His Best Teacher Award from the Courant Institute in 1970 presaged what many students would later confirm.

Rabin was elected to the American Academy of Arts and Sciences in 1975, the Israel Academy of Sciences and Humanities in 1982, the U.S. National Academy of Sciences as a Foreign Associate in 1984, the American Philosophical Society in 1988, the French Academy of Sciences as an Associé Étranger in 1995, and the Royal Society as a Foreign Member in 2007.

Dennis Shasha, a professor of computer science at New York University who studied with Rabin at Harvard starting in 1980, recalled Rabin’s algorithms course “was a challenge to all of us” and that Rabin “was a great raconteur and a great joker.”14 Ran Canetti, a professor at Boston University and ACM Fellow, provides an anecdote showing this: “One day (around 2005), he came over for dinner at our place with his daughter Tal, and started leafing through this book that I just got online, called ‘A million random digits.’ The book contained, surprise, lots of random digits. He then remarked in an offhand, dry tone, ‘They should post an errata volume,’ closed the book, and moved on to the next topic. It took us several seconds to figure out it was a joke . . . and a pretty good one.”6

Shasha dedicated his first puzzle book to Rabin as one of his three most influential teachers; in a later profile of great computer scientists, he assigned Rabin the epithet “the possibilities of chance.”

Rabin’s probabilistic work met resistance from some. Greg Morrisett, ACM Fellow, Dean and Vice Provost at Cornell Tech, recalled Rabin describing the reception of the Miller-Rabin test at dinner years later: Rabin “got lots of hate mail from computer scientists, mathematicians, etc., that he was pulling some sort of dirty trick.” Morrisett’s own verdict: “That’s a real hallmark of innovation: when you do something so revolutionary that people hate you for the result.”5

Rabin’s daughter, Tal—herself an accomplished cryptographer—captured something essential when she said in her eulogy: “You believed in simplicity and clarity in your results and in life. Everything was said clearly, there was no room for interpretation, and you never complicated situations.”7 That quality, call it determinism of conviction, in a career built on nondeterminism, was a theme through everything Rabin did.

ACM Fellow Barbara Grosz, a professor at Harvard, emphasized that clarity: “Michael’s expository talents transcended teaching. Several times when he introduced me in informal settings, I found myself wishing I could describe my research as clearly as he did. On other occasions, I noticed he described many other people’s work better than they did as well.”3

Turing Award Laureate Leslie G. Valiant summed it up: “Michael Rabin brought his singular combination of intellectual power, impeccable scientific taste and infectious excitement to the study of computer science. His love of mathematics was ever present with him. He has lasting impact through both his fundamental scientific contributions, as well as the openness of his personal interactions with others. Computer science is fortunate to have had Michael among its founding generation.”4

Rabin’s wife, Ruth—who headed the International Department of the State Attorney General’s office in Israel—predeceased him by four months after seventy-one years of marriage. He is survived by his daughters, Tal Rabin and Sharon Rabin-Margalioth, a professor of law; his son-in-law, Yoram Margalioth; five grandchildren, Danna Rabin Kelmer, Yael Rabin Kelmer, Jonathan Menachem Margalioth, Daniel Rabin Margalioth, and Roy Adam Rabin Margalioth; and one great-grandson, born in the days following his death.2
item.dc:creator: Simson Garfinkel
